dn16:1.1.2: that on one occasion the Blessed One was staying near Rājagaha on Vulture Peak Mountain.
dn16:1.1.3: And on that occasion, Ajātasattu Vedehiputta, the king of Magadha, wanted to attack the Vajjians.
dn16:1.1.5: “I will cut down these Vajjians—so mighty, so powerful! I will destroy these Vajjians! I will bring these Vajjians to ruin—these Vajjians!”
dn16:1.2.1: Then he addressed Vassakāra the brahman, the chief minister of Magadha:
dn16:1.2.2: “Come, brahman. Go to the Blessed One and, on arrival, show reverence with your head to his feet in my name and ask whether he is free from illness & affliction, is carefree, strong, & living in comfort, (saying,)
dn16:1.2.3: ‘Ajātasattu Vedehiputta, the king of Magadha, lord, shows reverence with his head to the Blessed One’s feet and asks whether you are free from illness & affliction, are carefree, strong, & living in comfort.’
dn16:1.2.5: ‘Lord, Ajātasattu Vedehiputta, the king of Magadha, wants to attack the Vajjians.
dn16:1.2.7: “I will cut down these Vajjians—so mighty, so powerful! I will destroy these Vajjians! I will bring these Vajjians to ruin—these Vajjians!”’
dn16:1.2.8: However the Blessed One answers, having grasped it well, report to me.
dn16:1.2.9: For Tathāgatas do not speak untruthfully.”
dn16:1.3.1: Responding, “As you say, sire,” to Ajātasattu Vedehiputta, the King of Magadha, Vassakāra the brahman, the chief minister of Magadha, had auspicious vehicles yoked, got into an auspicious vehicle, left Rājagaha with the auspicious vehicles and drove toward Vulture Peak Mountain.
dn16:1.3.2: Going in his vehicle as far as there was ground for a vehicle, getting down from his vehicle, he approached the Blessed One on foot.
dn16:1.3.3: On arrival, he exchanged courteous greetings with the Blessed One. After an exchange of friendly greetings & courtesies, he sat to one side. As he was sitting there, he told the Blessed One,
dn16:1.3.4: “Ajātasattu Vedehiputta, the king of Magadha, Master Gotama, shows reverence with his head to Master Gotama’s feet and asks whether you are free from illness & affliction, are carefree, strong, & living in comfort.
dn16:1.3.5: Master Gotama, Ajātasattu Vedehiputta, the king of Magadha, wants to attack the Vajjians.
dn16:1.3.7: ‘I will cut down these Vajjians—so mighty, so powerful! I will destroy these Vajjians! I will bring these Vajjians to ruin—these Vajjians!’”
dn16:1.4.1: Now on that occasion Ven. Ānanda was standing behind the Blessed One, fanning him.
dn16:1.4.2: So the Blessed One addressed Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:1.4.3: “Have you heard, Ānanda, that the Vajjians meet often and meet a great deal?”
dn16:1.4.4: “I have heard, lord, that the Vajjians meet often and meet a great deal.”
dn16:1.4.5: “As long as the Vajjians meet often and meet a great deal, Ānanda, their growth can be expected, not their decline.
dn16:1.4.6: “Have you heard, Ānanda, that the Vajjians meet in harmony, adjourn from their meetings in harmony, and conduct their Vajjian business in harmony?”
dn16:1.4.8: “As long as the Vajjians meet in harmony, adjourn from their meetings in harmony, and conduct their Vajjian business in harmony, Ānanda, their growth can be expected, not their decline.
dn16:1.4.9: “Have you heard, Ānanda, that the Vajjians neither decree what has been undecreed nor repeal what has been decreed, but conduct themselves, having undertaken the ancient Vajjian laws as they have been decreed?”
dn16:1.4.11: “As long as the Vajjians neither decree what has been undecreed nor repeal what has been decreed, but conduct themselves, having undertaken the ancient Vajjian laws as they have been decreed, Ānanda, their growth can be expected, not their decline.
dn16:1.4.12: “Have you heard, Ānanda, that the Vajjians honor, respect, venerate, and do homage to the Vajjian elders of the Vajjis, regarding them as worth listening to?”
dn16:1.4.14: “As long as the Vajjians honor, respect, venerate, and do homage to the Vajjian elders of the Vajjis, regarding them as worth listening to, Ānanda, their growth can be expected, not their decline.
dn16:1.4.15: “Have you heard, Ānanda, that the Vajjians do not roughly drag off women & girls of good families and take them captive?”
dn16:1.4.17: “As long as the Vajjians do not roughly drag off women & girls of good families and take them captive, Ānanda, their growth can be expected, not their decline.
dn16:1.4.18: “Have you heard, Ānanda, that the Vajjians honor, respect, venerate, and do homage to the Vajjian shrines, both inside (the city) and out, and that they do not let the righteous offerings done in the past and given in the past to those shrines fall into decline?”
dn16:1.4.20: “As long as the Vajjians honor, respect, venerate, and do homage to the Vajjian shrines, both inside (the city) and out, and do not let the righteous offerings done in the past and given in the past to those shrines fall into decline, Ānanda, their growth can be expected, not their decline.
dn16:1.4.21: “Have you heard, Ānanda, that righteous protection, watch, and guarding for arahants is well-provided by the Vajjians (with the thought,) ‘If there are any arahants who have yet to come to our domain, may they come; and may the arahants who have come to our domain live in comfort’?
dn16:1.4.23: “As long as righteous protection, watch, and guarding for arahants is well-provided by the Vajjians (with the thought,) ‘If there are any arahants who have yet to come to our domain, may they come; and may the arahants who have come to our domain live in comfort,’ Ānanda, their growth can be expected, not their decline.”
dn16:1.5.1: Then the Blessed One addressed Vassakāra the brahman, the chief minister of Magadha,
dn16:1.5.2: “Once, brahman, I was staying near Vesālī at the Sārandada shrine.
dn16:1.5.3: There I taught the Vajjians these seven conditions that lead to no decline.
dn16:1.5.4: As long as these seven conditions endure among the Vajjians, and as long as the Vajjians remain steadfast in these seven conditions, the Vajjians’ growth can be expected, not their decline.”
dn16:1.5.5: When this was said, Vassakāra the brahman, the chief minister of Magadha, said to the Blessed One,
dn16:1.5.6: “Master Gotama, even if the Vajjians are endowed with only one of these conditions that lead to no decline, the Vajjians’ growth can be expected, not their decline—
dn16:1.5.7: to say nothing of all seven.
dn16:1.5.8: Nothing can be done to the Vajjians by King Ajātasattu Vedehiputta, the king of Magadha, through force of arms—except by befriending them and sowing dissension (among them).
dn16:1.5.9: “Well, then, Master Gotama, we must go now. Many are our duties, many our responsibilities.”
dn16:1.5.10: “Then do, brahman, what you think it is now time to do.”
dn16:1.5.11: Then Vassakāra the brahman, the chief minister of Magadha, delighting in and approving of the Blessed One’s words, got up from his seat and left.
dn16:1.6.1: Not long after his departure, the Blessed One addressed Ven. Ānanda:
dn16:1.6.2: “Go, Ānanda. Have all the monks living in dependence on Rājagaha gather at the assembly hall.”
dn16:1.6.3: Responding, “As you say, lord,” to the Blessed One, Ven. Ānanda—having had all the monks living in dependence on Rājagaha gather at the assembly hall—approached the Blessed One. On arrival, having bowed down to him, he stood to one side. As he was standing there, he said to the Blessed One,
dn16:1.6.4: “The Saṅgha of monks has gathered, lord. May the Blessed One do what he thinks it is now time to do.”
dn16:1.6.5: Then the Blessed One, getting up from his seat, went to the assembly hall and, on arrival, sat down on the seat laid out.
dn16:1.6.6: Having sat down, he addressed the monks:
dn16:1.6.7: “Monks, I will teach you the seven conditions that lead to no decline.
dn16:1.6.8: Listen & pay close attention. I will speak.”
dn16:1.6.9: “As you say, lord,” the monks responded to the Blessed One.
dn16:1.6.11: “Monks, as long as the monks meet often, meet a great deal, their growth can be expected, not their decline.
dn16:1.6.12: “As long as the monks meet in harmony, adjourn from their meetings in harmony, and conduct Saṅgha business in harmony, their growth can be expected, not their decline.
dn16:1.6.13: “As long as the monks neither decree what has been undecreed nor repeal what has been decreed, but conduct themselves, having undertaken the training rules as they have been decreed, their growth can be expected, not their decline.
dn16:1.6.14: “As long as the monks honor, respect, venerate, and do homage to the elder monks—those with seniority who have long been ordained, the fathers of the Saṅgha, leaders of the Saṅgha—regarding them as worth listening to, their growth can be expected, not their decline.
dn16:1.6.15: “As long as the monks do not come under the sway of any arisen craving that leads to further-becoming, their growth can be expected, not their decline.
dn16:1.6.16: “As long as the monks see their own benefit in wilderness dwellings, their growth can be expected, not their decline.
dn16:1.6.17: “And as long as the monks each keep firmly in mind: ‘If there are any well-behaved companions in the holy life who have yet to come, may they come; and may the well-behaved companions in the holy life who have come live in comfort,’ their growth can be expected, not their decline.
dn16:1.6.18: “As long as these seven conditions endure among the monks, and as long as the monks remain steadfast in these seven conditions, the monks’ growth can be expected, not their decline.”4
dn16:1.7.1: “Monks, I will teach you seven further conditions that lead to no decline. Listen & pay close attention. I will speak.”
dn16:1.7.2: “As you say, lord,” the monks responded to the Blessed One.
dn16:1.7.4: “Monks, as long as the monks are not infatuated with (construction) work, do not delight in construction work, and are not committed to infatuation with construction work, the monks’ growth can be expected, not their decline.
dn16:1.7.5: “As long as the monks are not infatuated with gossip…
dn16:1.7.6: “As long as the monks are not infatuated with sleeping…
dn16:1.7.7: “As long as the monks are not infatuated with entanglement…
dn16:1.7.8: “As long as the monks are not infatuated with evil ambition and have not come under the sway of evil ambitions, the monks’ growth can be expected, not their decline.
dn16:1.7.9: “As long as the monks do not have evil friends, evil companions, and evil comrades, the monks’ growth can be expected, not their decline.
dn16:1.7.10: “And as long as the monks do not stop half-way with lower distinctions & achievements, the monks’ growth can be expected, not their decline.
dn16:1.8.1: “As long as these seven conditions endure among the monks, and as long as the monks remain steadfast in these seven conditions, the monks’ growth can be expected, not their decline.”
dn16:1.8.2: “Monks, I will teach you seven further conditions that lead to no decline. Listen & pay close attention. I will speak.” “As you say, lord,” the monks responded to the Blessed One. The Blessed One said:
dn16:1.8.3: “Monks, as long as the monks have conviction…
dn16:1.8.9: discernment, the monks’ growth can be expected, not their decline.6
dn16:1.8.10: “As long as these seven conditions endure among the monks, and as long as the monks remain steadfast in these seven conditions, the monks’ growth can be expected, not their decline.”
dn16:1.8.11: “Monks, I will teach you seven further conditions that lead to no decline. Listen & pay close attention. I will speak.”
dn16:1.8.12: “As you say, lord,” the monks responded to the Blessed One.
dn16:1.9.1: “Monks, as long as the monks develop mindfulness as a factor for awakening…
dn16:1.9.2: analysis of qualities as a factor for awakening…
dn16:1.9.3: persistence as a factor for awakening…
dn16:1.9.4: rapture as a factor for awakening…
dn16:1.9.5: calm as a factor for awakening…
dn16:1.9.6: concentration as a factor for awakening…
dn16:1.9.7: equanimity as a factor for awakening, the monks’ growth can be expected, not their decline.
dn16:1.10.1: “As long as these seven conditions endure among the monks, and as long as the monks remain steadfast in these seven conditions, the monks’ growth can be expected, not their decline.”
dn16:1.10.2: “Monks, I will teach you seven further conditions that lead to no decline. Listen & pay close attention. I will speak.”
dn16:1.10.3: “As you say, lord,” the monks responded to the Blessed One.
dn16:1.10.5: “Monks, as long as the monks develop the perception of inconstancy…
dn16:1.10.11: the perception of cessation, the monks’ growth can be expected, not their decline.8
dn16:1.10.12: “As long as these seven conditions endure among the monks, and as long as the monks remain steadfast in these seven conditions, the monks’ growth can be expected, not their decline.”
dn16:1.11.1: “Monks, I will teach you six further9 conditions that lead to no decline. Listen & pay close attention. I will speak.”
dn16:1.11.2: “As you say, lord,” the monks responded to the Blessed One.
dn16:1.11.4: “As long as the monks are set on bodily acts of good will with regard to their companions in the holy life, to their faces & behind their backs, the monks’ growth can be expected, not their decline.
dn16:1.11.5: “As long as the monks are set on verbal acts of good will with regard to their companions in the holy life, to their faces & behind their backs…
dn16:1.11.6: “As long as the monks are set on mental acts of good will with regard to their companions in the holy life, to their faces & behind their backs…
dn16:1.11.7: “As long as the monks, whatever righteous gains they may obtain in a righteous way—even if only the alms in their bowls—do not consume them alone, but consume them after sharing them in common with their virtuous companions in the holy life…
dn16:1.11.8: “As long as the monks—with reference to the virtues that are untorn, unbroken, unspotted, unsplattered, liberating, praised by the observant, ungrasped at, leading to concentration—dwell with their virtue in tune with that of their companions in the holy life, to their faces & behind their backs…
dn16:1.11.9: “And as long as the monks—with reference to the view that is noble, leading outward, that leads those who act in accordance with it to the right ending of suffering & stress—dwell with their view in tune with those of their companions in the holy life, to their faces & behind their backs, the monks’ growth can be expected, not their decline.
dn16:1.11.10: “As long as these six conditions endure among the monks, and as long as the monks remain steadfast in these six conditions, the monks’ growth can be expected, not their decline.”
dn16:1.12.1: While staying there near Rājagaha on Vulture Peak Mountain, the Blessed One often gave this Dhamma talk to the monks:
dn16:1.12.2: “Such is virtue, such is concentration, such is discernment.
dn16:1.12.3: Concentration nurtured with virtue is of great fruit, great reward.
dn16:1.12.4: Discernment nurtured with concentration is of great fruit, great reward.
dn16:1.12.5: The mind nurtured with discernment is rightly released from the effluents, i.e.,
dn16:1.12.6: the effluent of sensuality, the effluent of becoming, the effluent of ignorance.”
dn16:1.13.1: Then the Blessed One, having stayed near Rājagaha as long as he pleased, said to Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:1.13.2: “Come, Ānanda, we will head for Ambalaṭṭhikā.”
dn16:1.13.3: “As you say, lord,” Ven. Ānanda responded to the Blessed One.
dn16:1.13.4: Then the Blessed One, together with a large Saṅgha of monks, went to Ambalaṭṭhikā.
dn16:1.14.1: There he stayed near Ambalaṭṭhikā at the Royal Cottage.
dn16:1.14.2: While staying there near Ambalaṭṭhikā at the Royal Cottage, the Blessed One often gave this Dhamma talk to the monks:
dn16:1.14.3: “Such is virtue, such is concentration, such is discernment.
dn16:1.14.4: Concentration nurtured with virtue is of great fruit, great reward.
dn16:1.14.5: Discernment nurtured with concentration is of great fruit, great reward.
dn16:1.14.6: The mind nurtured with discernment is rightly released from the effluents, i.e.,
dn16:1.14.7: the effluent of sensuality, the effluent of becoming, the effluent of ignorance.”
dn16:1.15.1: Then the Blessed One, having stayed near Ambalaṭṭhikā as long as he pleased, said to Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:1.15.2: “Come, Ānanda, we will head for Nāḷandā.”
dn16:1.15.3: “As you say, lord,” Ven. Ānanda responded to the Blessed One.
dn16:1.15.4: Then the Blessed One, together with a large Saṅgha of monks, went to Nāḷandā.
dn16:1.15.5: There he stayed near Nāḷandā at the Pāvādika mango grove.
dn16:1.16.1: Then Ven. Sāriputta went to the Blessed One and, on arrival, having bowed down to him, sat to one side. As he was sitting there, Ven. Sāriputta said to the Blessed One,
dn16:1.16.2: “Lord, I have confidence in the Blessed One
dn16:1.16.3: that there neither has been nor will be nor is currently found a contemplative or brahman whose direct knowledge of self-awakening is greater than that of the Blessed One!”
dn16:1.16.4: “Grand is this bull-statement you have spoken, Sāriputta; categorical this lion’s roar you have roared:
dn16:1.16.5: ‘Lord, I have confidence in the Blessed One
dn16:1.16.6: that there neither has been nor will be nor is currently found a contemplative or brahman whose direct knowledge of self-awakening is greater than that of the Blessed One!’
dn16:1.16.7: So then, Sāriputta, have you encompassed with your awareness the awareness of all the worthy ones, the rightly self-awakened ones that have been in the past and known:
dn16:1.16.8: ‘Such was their virtue, such their Dhamma, such their discernment, such their (meditative) dwelling, such their release’?”
dn16:1.16.10: “Then have you encompassed with your awareness the awareness of all the worthy ones, the rightly self-awakened ones that will be in the future and known:
dn16:1.16.11: ‘Such will be their virtue, such their Dhamma, such their discernment, such their (meditative) dwelling, such their release’?”
dn16:1.16.13: “Then have you encompassed with your awareness my awareness—the awareness of the worthy one, the rightly self-awakened one in the present—and known:
dn16:1.16.14: ‘Such is his virtue, such his Dhamma, such his discernment, such his (meditative) dwelling, such his release’?”
dn16:1.16.16: “Then, Sāriputta, if you don’t have knowledge of the awareness of the worthy ones, the rightly self-awakened ones of the past, future, & present,
dn16:1.16.17: how is it that just now you spoke this grand bull-statement and roared this categorical lion’s roar:
dn16:1.16.18: ‘Lord, I have confidence in the Blessed One that there neither has been nor will be nor is currently found a contemplative or brahman whose direct knowledge of self-awakening is greater than that of the Blessed One’?”
dn16:1.17.1: “Lord, I don’t have knowledge of the awareness of the worthy ones, the rightly self-awakened ones of the past, future, & present,
dn16:1.17.2: but I have known the consistency of the Dhamma.
dn16:1.17.3: It’s as if there were a royal frontier city with strong ramparts, strong walls & arches, and a single gate.
dn16:1.17.4: In it would be a wise, competent, & intelligent gatekeeper to keep out those he didn’t know and to let in those he did.
dn16:1.17.5: Walking along the path encircling the city, he wouldn’t see a crack or an opening in the walls big enough for even a cat to slip through.
dn16:1.17.6: The thought would occur to him:
dn16:1.17.7: ‘Whatever large creatures enter or leave the city all enter or leave it through this gate.’
dn16:1.17.8: “In the same way, I have known the consistency of the Dhamma:
dn16:1.17.9: ‘All those who were worthy ones, the rightly self-awakened ones in the past awoke to the unexcelled right self-awakening after having abandoned the five hindrances—those defilements of awareness that weaken discernment—having well-established their minds in the four establishings of mindfulness and having developed, as they have come to be, the seven factors for awakening.
dn16:1.17.10: All those who will be worthy ones, the rightly self-awakened ones in the future will awaken to the unexcelled right self-awakening after having abandoned the five hindrances—those defilements of awareness that weaken discernment—having well-established their minds in the four establishings of mindfulness and having developed, as they have come to be, the seven factors for awakening.
dn16:1.17.11: The Blessed One who is now the worthy one, the rightly self-awakened one has awakened to the unexcelled right self-awakening after having abandoned the five hindrances—those defilements of awareness that weaken discernment—having well-established his mind in the four establishings of mindfulness and having developed, as they have come to be, the seven factors for awakening.”
dn16:1.18.1: While staying there near Nāḷandā at the Pāvādika mango grove, the Blessed One often gave this Dhamma talk to the monks:
dn16:1.18.2: “Such is virtue, such is concentration, such is discernment.
dn16:1.18.3: Concentration nurtured with virtue is of great fruit, great reward.
dn16:1.18.4: Discernment nurtured with concentration is of great fruit, great reward.
dn16:1.18.5: The mind nurtured with discernment is rightly released from the effluents, i.e.,
dn16:1.18.6: the effluent of sensuality, the effluent of becoming, the effluent of ignorance.”
dn16:1.19.1: Then the Blessed One, having stayed near Nāḷandā as long as he pleased, said to Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:1.19.2: “Come, Ānanda, we will head for Pāṭali Village.”
dn16:1.19.3: “As you say, lord,” Ven. Ānanda responded to the Blessed One.
dn16:1.19.4: Then the Blessed One, together with a large Saṅgha of monks, went to Pāṭali Village.
dn16:1.20.1: The lay followers of Pāṭali Village heard,
dn16:1.20.2: “The Blessed One, they say, has reached Pāṭali Village.” So they went to the Blessed One and, on arrival, having bowed down to him, sat to one side. As they were sitting there, they said to him,
dn16:1.20.3: “Lord, may the Blessed One acquiesce to (the use of) the rest-house hall.”
dn16:1.20.4: The Blessed One acquiesced with silence.
dn16:1.21.1: Sensing his acquiescence, the lay followers of Pāṭali Village got up from their seats and, bowing down to him and circumambulating him, went to the rest-house hall. On arrival, they spread it all over with felt rugs, arranged seats, set out a water vessel, and raised an oil lamp. Then they went to the Blessed One and, on arrival, having bowed down to him, stood to one side. As they were standing there, they said to him,
dn16:1.21.2: “Lord, the rest-house hall has been covered all over with felt rugs, seats have been arranged, a water vessel has been set out, and an oil lamp raised.
dn16:1.21.3: May the Blessed One do what he thinks it is now time to do.”
dn16:1.22.1: So the Blessed One, in the evening, after adjusting his lower robe and taking his bowl & outer robe, went together with the Saṅgha of monks to the rest-house hall. On arrival he washed his feet, entered the hall, and sat with his back to the central post, facing east.
dn16:1.22.2: The Saṅgha of monks washed their feet, entered the hall, and sat with their backs to the western wall, facing east, ranged around the Blessed One.
dn16:1.22.3: The lay followers of Pāṭali Village washed their feet, entered the hall, and sat with their backs to the eastern wall, facing west, ranged around the Blessed One.
dn16:1.23.1: Then the Blessed One addressed the lay followers of Pāṭali Village,
dn16:1.23.2: “Householders, there are these five drawbacks coming from an unvirtuous person’s defect in virtue.
dn16:1.23.4: “There is the case where an unvirtuous person, defective in virtue, by reason of heedlessness undergoes the loss/confiscation of great wealth.
dn16:1.23.5: This is the first drawback coming from an unvirtuous person’s defect in virtue.
dn16:1.23.6: “And further, the bad reputation of the unvirtuous person, defective in virtue, gets spread about.
dn16:1.23.7: This is the second drawback coming from an unvirtuous person’s defect in virtue.
dn16:1.23.8: “And further, whatever assembly the unvirtuous person, defective in virtue, approaches—whether of noble warriors, brahmans, householders, or contemplatives—he/she does so without confidence & abashed.
dn16:1.23.9: This is the third drawback coming from an unvirtuous person’s defect in virtue.
dn16:1.23.10: “And further, the unvirtuous person, defective in virtue, dies confused.
dn16:1.23.11: This is the fourth drawback coming from an unvirtuous person’s defect in virtue.
dn16:1.23.12: “And further, the unvirtuous person, defective in virtue—on the break-up of the body, after death—reappears in a plane of deprivation, a bad destination, a lower realm, hell.
dn16:1.23.13: This is the fifth drawback coming from an unvirtuous person’s defect in virtue.
dn16:1.23.14: “These, householders, are the five drawbacks coming from an unvirtuous person’s defect in virtue.
dn16:1.24.1: “Householders, there are these five rewards coming from a virtuous person’s consummation in virtue.
dn16:1.24.3: “There is the case where a virtuous person, consummate in virtue, by reason of heedfulness acquires a great mass of wealth.
dn16:1.24.4: This is the first reward coming from a virtuous person’s consummation in virtue.
dn16:1.24.5: “And further, the fine reputation of the virtuous person, consummate in virtue, gets spread about.
dn16:1.24.6: This is the second reward coming from a virtuous person’s consummation in virtue.
dn16:1.24.7: “And further, whatever assembly the virtuous person, consummate in virtue, approaches—whether of noble warriors, brahmans, householders, or contemplatives—he/she does so with confidence & unabashed.
dn16:1.24.8: This is the third reward coming from a virtuous person’s consummation in virtue.
dn16:1.24.9: “And further, the virtuous person, consummate in virtue, dies unconfused.
dn16:1.24.10: This is the fourth reward coming from a virtuous person’s consummation in virtue.
dn16:1.24.11: “And further, the virtuous person, consummate in virtue—on the break-up of the body, after death—reappears in a good destination, a heavenly world.
dn16:1.24.12: This is the fifth reward coming from a virtuous person’s consummation in virtue.
dn16:1.24.13: “These, householders, are the five rewards coming from a virtuous person’s consummation in virtue.”
dn16:1.25.1: Then the Blessed One—having instructed, urged, roused, & encouraged the lay followers of Pāṭali Village for a large part of the night with Dhamma talk—dismissed them, saying,
dn16:1.25.2: “The night is far gone, householders. Do what you think it is now time to do.”
dn16:1.25.3: Responding, “As you say, lord,” to the Blessed One, the lay followers of Pāṭali Village got up from their seats and, bowing down to him and circumambulating him, left.
dn16:1.25.4: Then the Blessed One, not long after they had left, entered an empty building.
dn16:1.26.1: Now on that occasion, Sunidha & Vassakāra, the chief ministers of Magadha, were building a city at Pāṭali Village to preempt the Vajjians.
dn16:1.26.2: And on that occasion many devas by the thousands were occupying sites in Pāṭali Village.
dn16:1.26.3: In the area where devas of great influence occupied sites, there the minds of the king’s royal ministers of great influence were inclined to build their homes.
dn16:1.27.1: In the area where devas of middling influence occupied sites, there the minds of the king’s royal ministers of middling influence were inclined to build their homes.
dn16:1.27.2: In the area where devas of low influence occupied sites, there the minds of the king’s royal ministers of low influence were inclined to build their homes.
dn16:1.27.3: The Blessed One, with the divine eye—purified and surpassing the human—saw those devas by the thousands occupying sites in Pāṭali Village.
dn16:1.27.4: Then, getting up in the last watch of the night, the Blessed One addressed Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:1.27.5: “Ānanda, who is building a city at Pāṭali Village?”
dn16:1.27.6: “Sunidha & Vassakāra, the chief ministers of Magadha, lord, are building a city at Pāṭali Village to preempt the Vajjians.”
dn16:1.28.1: “Ānanda, it’s as if they had consulted the Devas of the Thirty-three:
dn16:1.28.2: That’s how Sunidha & Vassakāra, the chief ministers of Magadha, are building a city at Pāṭali Village to preempt the Vajjians.
dn16:1.28.3: “Just now, Ānanda—with the divine eye—purified and surpassing the human—I saw many devas by the thousands occupying sites in Pāṭali Village.
dn16:1.28.4: In the area where devas of great influence occupy sites, there the minds of the king’s royal ministers of great influence are inclined to build their homes.
dn16:1.28.5: In the area where devas of middling influence occupy sites, there the minds of the king’s royal ministers of middling influence are inclined to build their homes.
dn16:1.28.6: In the area where devas of low influence occupy sites, there the minds of the king’s royal ministers of low influence are inclined to build their homes.
dn16:1.28.7: “Ānanda, as far as the sphere of the Ariyans extends, as far as merchants’ roads extend, this will be the supreme city:
dn16:1.28.8: Pāṭaliputta, where the seedpods of the Pāṭali plant break open. There will be three dangers for Pāṭaliputta:
dn16:1.28.9: from fire, from water, or from the breaking of alliances.”
dn16:1.29.1: Then Sunidha & Vassakāra, the chief ministers of Magadha, went to the Blessed One and, on arrival, exchanged courteous greetings with him.
dn16:1.29.2: After an exchange of friendly greetings & courtesies, they stood to one side. As they were standing there, they said to him,
dn16:1.29.3: “May Master Gotama acquiesce to our meal today, together with the Saṅgha of monks.”
dn16:1.29.4: The Blessed One acquiesced with silence.
dn16:1.30.1: Then Sunidha & Vassakāra, the chief ministers of Magadha, understanding the Blessed One’s acquiescence, went to their rest-house. On arrival, after having exquisite staple & non-staple food prepared in their rest-house, they announced the time to the Blessed One:
dn16:1.30.2: “It’s time, Master Gotama. The meal is ready.”
dn16:1.30.3: Then the Blessed One, early in the morning, adjusted his lower robe and—taking his bowl & outer robe—went together with the Saṅgha of monks to the rest-house of Sunidha & Vassakāra, the chief ministers of Magadha. On arrival, he sat down on the seat laid out.
dn16:1.30.4: Sunidha & Vassakāra, with their own hands, served & satisfied the Saṅgha of monks, with the Buddha at its head, with exquisite staple & non-staple food.
dn16:1.30.5: Then, when the Blessed One had finished his meal and had rinsed his bowl & hands, Sunidha & Vassakāra, taking a low seat, sat to one side.
dn16:1.31.1: As they were sitting there, the Blessed One gave his approval with these verses:
dn16:1.31.3: a wise person makes his dwelling,
dn16:1.31.4: —there providing food for the virtuous, the restrained,
dn16:1.31.5: the restrained, leaders of the holy life—
dn16:1.31.7: They, receiving honor, will honor him;
dn16:1.31.10: As a result, they will feel sympathy for him,
dn16:1.31.11: like that of a mother for her child, her son.
dn16:1.31.12: A person with whom the devas sympathize
dn16:1.31.14: Then the Blessed One, having given his approval to Sunidha & Vassakāra with these verses, got up from his seat and left.
dn16:1.32.1: And on that occasion, Sunidha & Vassakāra followed right after the Blessed One, (thinking,)
dn16:1.32.2: “By whichever gate Gotama the contemplative departs today, that will be called the Gotama Gate.
dn16:1.32.3: And by whichever ford he crosses over the Ganges River, that will be called the Gotama Ford.”
dn16:1.32.4: So the gate by which the Blessed One departed was called the Gotama Gate.
dn16:1.32.5: Then he went to the Ganges River.
dn16:1.33.1: Now on that occasion the Ganges River was full up to the banks, so that a crow could drink from it.
dn16:1.33.2: Some people were searching for boats; some were searching for floats; some were binding rafts in hopes of going from this shore to the other.
dn16:1.33.3: So the Blessed One—just as a strong man might extend his flexed arm or flex his extended arm—disappeared from the near bank of the Ganges River and reappeared on the far bank together with the Saṅgha of monks.
dn16:1.34.1: He saw that some people were searching for boats; some were searching for floats; some were binding rafts in hopes of going from this shore to the other.
dn16:1.34.2: Then, on realizing the significance of that, the Blessed One on that occasion exclaimed:
dn16:1.34.3: Those who cross the foaming flood,
dn16:1.34.4: having made a bridge, avoiding the swamps
dn16:2.1.1: Then the Blessed One said to Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:2.1.2: “Come, Ānanda, we will head for Koṭi Village.”
dn16:2.1.3: “As you say, lord,” Ven. Ānanda responded to the Blessed One.
dn16:2.1.4: Then the Blessed One, together with a large Saṅgha of monks, went to Koṭi Village.
dn16:2.1.5: There he stayed near Koṭi Village.
dn16:2.2.1: And there he addressed the monks:
dn16:2.2.2: “It’s through not awakening to or penetrating four noble truths, monks, that we have transmigrated and wandered on for such a long time, you & I.
dn16:2.2.4: “It’s through not awakening to or penetrating the noble truth of stress that we have transmigrated and wandered on for such a long time, you & I.
dn16:2.2.5: It’s through not awakening to or penetrating the noble truth of the origination of stress…
dn16:2.2.6: It’s through not awakening to or penetrating the noble truth of the cessation of stress…
dn16:2.2.7: It’s through not awakening to or penetrating the noble truth of the path of practice leading to cessation of stress that we have transmigrated and wandered on for such a long time, you & I.
dn16:2.2.8: “(But now,) this noble truth of stress has been awakened to & penetrated, the noble truth of the origination of stress has been awakened to & penetrated, the noble truth of the cessation of stress has been awakened to & penetrated, the noble truth of the path of practice leading to cessation of stress has been awakened to & penetrated. Craving for becoming has been crushed; the guide to becoming [i.e., clinging] is ended. There now is no further-becoming.”
dn16:2.3.1: That is what the Blessed One said.
dn16:2.3.2: Having said it, the Teacher, the One Well-Gone, said further:
dn16:2.3.5: we have wandered a long time
dn16:2.3.8: the guide to becoming is removed,
dn16:2.3.9: crushed is the root of suffering & stress.
dn16:2.4.1: While staying there near Koṭi Village, the Blessed One often gave this Dhamma talk to the monks:
dn16:2.4.2: “Such is virtue, such is concentration, such is discernment.
dn16:2.4.3: Concentration nurtured with virtue is of great fruit, great reward.
dn16:2.4.4: Discernment nurtured with concentration is of great fruit, great reward.
dn16:2.4.5: The mind nurtured with discernment is rightly released from the effluents, i.e.,
dn16:2.4.6: the effluent of sensuality, the effluent of becoming, the effluent of ignorance.”
dn16:2.5.1: Then the Blessed One, having stayed near Koṭi Village as long as he pleased, said to Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:2.5.2: “Come, Ānanda, we will head for Nādikā.”
dn16:2.5.3: “As you say, lord,” Ven. Ānanda responded to the Blessed One.
dn16:2.5.4: Then the Blessed One, together with a large Saṅgha of monks, went to Nādikā.
dn16:2.5.5: There he stayed near Nādikā at the Brick Hall.
dn16:2.6.1: Then Ven. Ānanda went to the Blessed One and, on arrival, having bowed down to him, sat to one side. As he was sitting there, Ven. Ānanda said to the Blessed One,
dn16:2.6.2: “Lord, the monk named Sāḷha has died in Nādikā. What is his destination, what his future state?
dn16:2.6.3: The nun named Nandā has died in Nādikā. What is her destination, what her future state?
dn16:2.6.4: The male lay-follower named Sudatta has died in Nādikā. What is his destination, what his future state?
dn16:2.6.5: The female lay-follower named Sujātā has died in Nādikā. What is her destination, what her future state?
dn16:2.6.13: Subhaṭa has died in Nādikā. What is his destination, what his future state?”
dn16:2.7.1: “Ānanda, the monk Sāḷha, with the ending of effluents, dwelt in the effluent-free awareness-release and discernment-release, having directly known and realized them for himself right in the here-and-now.
dn16:2.7.2: The nun Nandā, with the ending of the five lower fetters,16 has spontaneously arisen (in the Pure Abodes,) there to be totally unbound, destined never again to return from that world.
dn16:2.7.3: Sudatta the male lay-follower, with the ending of [the first] three fetters, and with the attenuation of passion, aversion, & delusion, is a once-returner, who—on returning only once more to this world—will put an end to stress.
dn16:2.7.4: Sujātā the female lay-follower, with the ending of [the first] three fetters, is a stream-winner, never again destined for states of destitution, certain, headed for self-awakening.
dn16:2.7.12: Subhaṭa the male lay-follower, with the ending of the five lower fetters, have spontaneously arisen (in the Pure Abodes,) there to be totally unbound, destined never again to return from that world.
dn16:2.7.13: “Ānanda, more than 50 lay-followers who have died in Nādikā, with the ending of the five lower fetters, have spontaneously arisen (in the Pure Abodes,) there to be totally unbound, destined never again to return from that world.
dn16:2.7.14: 96 lay-followers who have died in Nādikā, with the ending of [the first] three fetters, and with the attenuation of passion, aversion, & delusion, are once-returners, who—on returning only once more to this world—will put an end to stress.
dn16:2.7.15: 510 lay-followers who have died in Nādikā, with the ending of [the first] three fetters, are stream-winners, never again destined for states of destitution, certain, headed for self-awakening.
dn16:2.8.1: “It’s not amazing, Ānanda, that anyone who has become a human being would die.
dn16:2.8.2: But if with every death you approach the Tathāgata and ask about this matter, that would be wearisome for him.
dn16:2.8.3: “Therefore, Ānanda, I will teach you the Dhamma-discourse called the Dhamma-mirror, endowed with which a disciple of the noble ones, if he/she so desires, may predict for him or herself:
dn16:2.8.4: ‘Hell is ended for me; animal wombs are ended; the state of the hungry ghosts is ended; planes of deprivation, the bad destinations, the lower realms are ended! I am a stream-winner, never again destined for the lower realms, certain, headed for self-awakening!’
dn16:2.9.1: “And what is the Dhamma-mirror Dhamma-discourse, endowed with which a disciple of the noble ones, if he/she so desires, may predict for him or herself:
dn16:2.9.2: ‘Hell is ended for me; animal wombs are ended; the state of the hungry ghosts is ended; planes of deprivation, the bad destinations, the lower realms are ended! I am a stream-winner, never again destined for the lower realms, certain, headed for self-awakening’?
dn16:2.9.3: “There is the case where the disciple of the noble ones is endowed with verified confidence in the Awakened One:
dn16:2.9.4: ‘Indeed, the Blessed One is worthy & rightly self-awakened, consummate in clear-knowing & conduct, well-gone, an expert with regard to the cosmos, unexcelled trainer of people fit to be tamed, teacher of devas & human beings, awakened, blessed.’
dn16:2.9.5: “He/she is endowed with verified confidence in the Dhamma:
dn16:2.9.6: ‘The Dhamma is well taught by the Blessed One, to be seen here & now, timeless, inviting verification, pertinent, to be experienced by the observant for themselves.’
dn16:2.9.7: “He/she is endowed with verified confidence in the Saṅgha:
dn16:2.9.8: ‘The Saṅgha of the Blessed One’s disciples who have practiced well… who have practiced straight-forwardly… who have practiced methodically… who have practiced masterfully—in other words, the four types of noble disciples when taken as pairs, the eight when taken as individual types—they are the Saṅgha of the Blessed One’s disciples: deserving of gifts, deserving of hospitality, deserving of offerings, deserving of respect, the incomparable field of merit for the world.’”
dn16:2.9.9: “He/she is endowed with virtues that are appealing to the noble ones: untorn, unbroken, unspotted, unsplattered, liberating, praised by the observant, ungrasped at, leading to concentration.
dn16:2.9.10: “This, Ānanda, is the Dhamma-mirror Dhamma-discourse, endowed with which a disciple of the noble ones, if he/she so desires, may predict for him or herself:
dn16:2.9.11: ‘Hell is ended for me; animal wombs are ended; the state of the hungry ghosts is ended; planes of deprivation, the bad destinations, the lower realms are ended! I am a stream-winner, never again destined for the lower realms, certain, headed for self-awakening!’”
dn16:2.10.1: While staying there near Nādikā in the Brick House, the Blessed One often gave this Dhamma talk to the monks:
dn16:2.10.2: “Such is virtue, such is concentration, such is discernment.
dn16:2.10.3: Concentration nurtured with virtue is of great fruit, great reward.
dn16:2.10.4: Discernment nurtured with concentration is of great fruit, great reward.
dn16:2.10.5: The mind nurtured with discernment is rightly released from effluents, i.e.,
dn16:2.10.6: the effluent of sensuality, the effluent of becoming, the effluent of ignorance.”
dn16:2.11.1: Then the Blessed One, having stayed near Nādikā as long as he pleased, said to Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:2.11.2: “Come, Ānanda, we will head for Vesālī.”
dn16:2.11.3: “As you say, lord,” Ven. Ānanda responded to the Blessed One.
dn16:2.11.4: Then the Blessed One, together with a large Saṅgha of monks, went to Vesālī.
dn16:2.11.5: There he stayed near Vesālī in Ambapālī’s grove.
dn16:2.12.1: And there he addressed the monks:
dn16:2.12.3: This is our instruction to you all.
dn16:2.12.4: And how is a monk mindful?
dn16:2.12.5: There is the case where a monk remains focused on the body in & of itself—ardent, alert, & mindful—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.
dn16:2.12.8: mental qualities in & of themselves—ardent, alert, & mindful—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.
dn16:2.12.9: This is how a monk is mindful.
dn16:2.13.2: When going forward & returning, he makes himself alert; when looking toward & looking away… when bending & extending his limbs… when carrying his outer cloak, his upper robe, & his bowl… when eating, drinking, chewing, & savoring… when urinating & defecating… when walking, standing, sitting, falling asleep, waking up, talking, & remaining silent, he makes himself alert.
dn16:2.13.3: This is how a monk is alert.
dn16:2.13.5: This is our instruction to you all.”
dn16:2.14.1: Then Ambapālī the courtesan heard, “The Blessed One, they say, has arrived at Vesālī and is staying near Vesālī in my mango [amba] grove!”
dn16:2.14.2: Then, having auspicious vehicles yoked, she got into an auspicious vehicle, left Vesālī with the auspicious vehicles and drove toward her own garden [the grove].
dn16:2.14.3: Going in her vehicle as far as there was ground for a vehicle, getting down from her vehicle, she approached the Blessed One on foot. On arrival, having bowed down to him, she sat to one side.
dn16:2.14.4: As she was sitting there, the Blessed One instructed, urged, roused, & encouraged her with a talk on Dhamma.
dn16:2.14.5: Then Ambapālī the courtesan—instructed, urged, roused, & encouraged with the Blessed One’s talk on Dhamma—said to him,
dn16:2.14.6: “Lord, may the Blessed One acquiesce to my meal tomorrow, together with the Saṅgha of monks.”
dn16:2.14.7: The Blessed One acquiesced with silence.
dn16:2.14.8: Then Ambapālī the courtesan, understanding the Blessed One’s acquiescence, got up from her seat and, bowing down to him and circumambulating him, left.
dn16:2.15.1: Then the Licchavis of Vesālī heard, “The Blessed One, they say, has arrived at Vesālī and is staying near Vesālī in Ambapālī’s grove!”
dn16:2.15.2: Then, having auspicious vehicles yoked, the Licchavis got into the auspicious vehicles, and left Vesālī with the auspicious vehicles.
dn16:2.15.3: Now, some of those Licchavis were dark blue, with dark blue complexions, dark blue clothing, & dark blue ornaments. Some of those Licchavis were yellow, with yellow complexions, yellow clothing, & yellow ornaments. Some of those Licchavis were red, with red complexions, red clothing, & red ornaments. Some of those Licchavis were white, with white complexions, white clothing, & white ornaments.
dn16:2.16.1: Then Ambapālī the courtesan, driving axle to axle, wheel to wheel, yoke to yoke, brought those young Licchavis to a halt.
dn16:2.16.2: So those Licchavis said to her,
dn16:2.16.3: “Hey, Ambapālī, what are you doing—driving axle to axle, wheel to wheel, yoke to yoke—bringing the young Licchavis to a halt?”
dn16:2.16.4: “Because, young masters, I have invited the Blessed One, together with the Saṅgha of monks, for tomorrow’s meal!”
dn16:2.16.6: “Even if the masters gave me Vesālī with all its revenue, I still wouldn’t give up such a great meal!”
dn16:2.16.7: Then the Licchavis snapped their fingers:
dn16:2.16.8: “How we’ve been defeated by Little Ambapālī! How we’ve been cheated by Little Ambapālī!”
dn16:2.16.9: Then the Licchavis set out for Ambapālī’s grove.
dn16:2.17.1: The Blessed One saw them coming from afar and,
dn16:2.17.2: on seeing them, he said to the monks,
dn16:2.17.3: “Those monks who have never before seen the Devas of the Thirty-three, look at the assembly of Licchavis!
dn16:2.17.4: Gaze at the assembly of Licchavis!
dn16:2.17.6: as like the assembly of the Thirty-three!”
dn16:2.18.1: Then the Licchavis, going in their vehicles as far as there was ground for vehicles, got down from their vehicles and approached the Blessed One on foot. On arrival, having bowed down to him, they sat to one side.
dn16:2.18.2: As they were sitting there, the Blessed One instructed, urged, roused, & encouraged them with a talk on Dhamma.
dn16:2.18.3: Then the Licchavis—instructed, urged, roused, and encouraged with the Blessed One’s talk on Dhamma—said to him,
dn16:2.18.4: “Lord, may the Blessed One acquiesce to our meal tomorrow, together with the Saṅgha of monks.”
dn16:2.18.6: “Licchavis, I have already acquiesced to Ambapālī the courtesan’s meal tomorrow.”
dn16:2.18.7: Then the Licchavis snapped their fingers:
dn16:2.18.8: “How we’ve been defeated by Little Ambapālī! How we’ve been cheated by Little Ambapālī!”
dn16:2.18.9: Then the Licchavis, delighting in and approving of the Blessed One’s words, got up from their seats and, bowing down to him and circumambulating him, left.
dn16:2.19.1: Then Ambapālī the courtesan, at the end of the night—after having exquisite staple & non-staple food prepared in her own garden—announced the time to the Blessed One:
dn16:2.19.2: “It’s time, lord. The meal is ready.”
dn16:2.19.3: Then the Blessed One, early in the morning, adjusted his lower robe and—taking his bowl & outer robe—went together with the Saṅgha of monks to Ambapālī the courtesan’s meal offering. On arrival, he sat down on the seat laid out.
dn16:2.19.4: Ambapālī the courtesan, with her own hands, served & satisfied the Saṅgha of monks, with the Buddha at its head, with exquisite staple & non-staple food.
dn16:2.19.5: Then, when the Blessed One had finished his meal and had rinsed his bowl & hands, Ambapālī the courtesan, taking a low seat, sat to one side.
dn16:2.19.6: As she was sitting there, she said to the Blessed One,
dn16:2.19.7: “Lord, I give this garden to the Saṅgha of monks with the Buddha at its head.”
dn16:2.19.8: The the Blessed One accepted the garden.
dn16:2.19.9: Then—having instructed, urged, roused, & encouraged Ambapālī the courtesan with a talk on Dhamma—got up from his seat and left.
dn16:2.20.1: While staying there near Vesālī in Ambapālī’s grove, the Blessed One often gave this Dhamma talk to the monks:
dn16:2.20.2: “Such is virtue, such is concentration, such is discernment.
dn16:2.20.3: Concentration nurtured with virtue is of great fruit, great reward.
dn16:2.20.4: Discernment nurtured with concentration is of great fruit, great reward.
dn16:2.20.5: The mind nurtured with discernment is rightly released from the effluents, i.e.,
dn16:2.20.6: the effluent of sensuality, the effluent of becoming, the effluent of ignorance.”
dn16:2.21.1: Then the Blessed One, having stayed in Ambapālī’s grove as long as he pleased, said to Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:2.21.2: “Come, Ānanda, we will head for Veḷuva Village.”
dn16:2.21.3: “As you say, lord,” Ven. Ānanda responded to the Blessed One.
dn16:2.21.4: Then the Blessed One, together with a large Saṅgha of monks, went to Veḷuva Village.
dn16:2.21.5: There he stayed near Veḷuva Village.
dn16:2.22.1: And there he addressed the monks:
dn16:2.22.2: “Come, monks, enter the Rains retreat around Vesālī with your friends, acquaintances, & eating companions.
dn16:2.22.3: As for me, I will enter the Rains retreat right here near Veḷuva Village.
dn16:2.22.4: Responding, “As you say, lord,” to the Blessed One, the monks entered the Rains retreat around Vesālī with their friends, acquaintances, & eating companions.
dn16:2.22.5: The Blessed One entered the Rains retreat right there near Veḷuva Village.
dn16:2.23.1: Then in the Blessed One, when he had entered the Rains retreat, there arose a severe disease with intense pains & deadly.
dn16:2.23.2: But the Blessed One endured it—mindful, alert, & not struck down by it.
dn16:2.23.4: “It would not be proper for me to totally unbind without having addressed my attendants or taken leave of the Saṅgha of monks.
dn16:2.23.5: Why don’t I, bending back this disease with persistence, keep determining the fabrications of life?”
dn16:2.24.1: So the Blessed One, bending back the disease with persistence, kept determining the fabrications of life.
dn16:2.24.3: Then the Blessed One—having recovered from being ill, not long recovered from the illness—went out of the dwelling and sat down on a seat laid out behind the dwelling.
dn16:2.24.4: Then Ven. Ānanda went to the Blessed One and, on arrival, having bowed down to him, sat to one side. As he was sitting there, he said to the Blessed One,
dn16:2.24.5: “What a happy sight to see the Blessed One’s comfort! What a happy sight to see the Blessed One’s being at ease! Because of the Blessed One’s illness my own body felt as if it were drugged. I lost my bearings. Things were unclear to me.
dn16:2.24.6: Yet I still took a measure of reassurance in the thought
dn16:2.24.7: that the Blessed One would not totally unbind as long as he hadn’t given at least some pronouncement concerning the Saṅgha of monks.”
dn16:2.25.1: “What more does the Saṅgha of monks want from me, Ānanda?
dn16:2.25.2: I have taught the Dhamma without making an inside or outside (version).
dn16:2.25.3: The Tathāgata has no closed fist with regard to teachings.
dn16:2.25.5: ‘I will govern the Saṅgha of monks,’ or ‘The Saṅgha of monks looks to me,’ he should give some pronouncement concerning the Saṅgha of monks.
dn16:2.25.6: But the thought doesn’t occur to the Tathāgata that
dn16:2.25.7: ‘I will govern the Saṅgha of monks,’ or ‘The Saṅgha of monks looks to me.’
dn16:2.25.8: So why should he give some pronouncement concerning the Saṅgha of monks?
dn16:2.25.9: “I am now aged, Ānanda: old, elderly, advanced in years, having come to the last stage of life,
dn16:2.25.11: Just as an old cart is kept going with the help of bamboo strips,
dn16:2.25.12: the Tathāgata’s body is kept going with the help of bamboo strips, as it were.
dn16:2.25.13: When the Tathāgata—not attending to any theme at all, and with the cessation of certain feelings—enters & remains in the theme-less concentration of awareness, that is when his body is more at ease.
dn16:2.26.1: “So, Ānanda, you should all live with yourselves as your island, yourselves as your refuge, with no other as your refuge; with the Dhamma as your island, the Dhamma as your refuge, with no other as your refuge.
dn16:2.26.2: And how does a monk live with himself as his island, himself as his refuge, with no other as his refuge; with the Dhamma as his island, the Dhamma as his refuge, with no other as his refuge?
dn16:2.26.3: There is the case where a monk remains focused on the body in & of itself—ardent, alert, & mindful—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.
dn16:2.26.4: He remains focused on feelings in & of themselves…
dn16:2.26.6: mental qualities in & of themselves—ardent, alert, & mindful—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.
dn16:2.26.7: This is how a monk lives with himself as his island, himself as his refuge, with no other as his refuge; with the Dhamma as his island, the Dhamma as his refuge, with no other as his refuge.
dn16:2.26.8: For those who, now or when I am gone, live with themselves as their island, themselves as their refuge, with no other as their refuge; with the Dhamma as their island, the Dhamma as their refuge, not with another as their refuge, will be my foremost monks: those who are desirous of training.”
dn16:3.1.1: Then the Blessed One, early in the morning, adjusted his lower robe and—taking his bowl & outer robe—went into Vesālī for alms.
dn16:3.1.2: Then, having gone for alms in Vesālī, after the meal, returning from his alms round, he addressed Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:3.1.4: We will go to the Pāvāla shrine for the day’s abiding.”
dn16:3.1.5: Responding, “As you say, lord,” to the Blessed One, Ven. Ānanda followed along behind the Blessed One, carrying the sitting cloth.
dn16:3.2.1: Then the Blessed One went to the Pāvāla shrine and, on arrival, sat down on the seat laid out.
dn16:3.2.3: Seated, the Blessed One addressed Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:3.2.4: “Vesālī is refreshing, Ānanda. Refreshing, too, are the Udena shrine, the Gotamaka shrine, the Sattamba shrine, the ManySon shrine, the Sāranda shrine, the Pāvāla shrine.23
dn16:3.3.1: “Anyone, Ānanda, in whom the four bases of power are developed, pursued, given a means of transport, given a grounding, steadied, consolidated, and well-undertaken, could—if he wanted—remain for an eon or the remainder of an eon.
dn16:3.3.2: In the Tathāgata, Ānanda, the four bases of power are developed, pursued, given a means of transport, given a grounding, steadied, consolidated, and well-undertaken. He could—if he wanted—remain for an eon or the remainder of an eon.”
dn16:3.4.1: But Ven. Ānanda—even when the Blessed One had given such a blatant sign, such a blatant hint—wasn’t able to understand his meaning.
dn16:3.4.2: He didn’t beg of him, “Lord, may the Blessed One remain for an eon.
dn16:3.4.3: May the One Well-Gone remain for an eon—for the benefit of the many, for the happiness of the many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of human beings & devas.” It was as if his mind were possessed by Māra.
dn16:3.5.2: A third time, the Blessed One addressed Ven. Ānanda, “Vesālī is refreshing, Ānanda.
dn16:3.5.3: Refreshing, too, are the Udena shrine, the Gotamaka shrine, the Sattamba shrine, the ManySon shrine, the Sāranda shrine, the Pāvāla shrine.
dn16:3.5.4: “Anyone, Ānanda, in whom the four bases of power are developed, pursued, given a means of transport, given a grounding, steadied, consolidated, and well-undertaken, could—if he wanted—remain for an eon or the remainder of an eon.
dn16:3.5.5: In the Tathāgata, Ānanda, the four bases of power are developed, pursued, given a means of transport, given a grounding, steadied, consolidated, and well-undertaken. He could—if he wanted—remain for an eon or the remainder of an eon.”
dn16:3.5.6: But Ven. Ānanda—even when the Blessed One had given such a blatant sign, such a blatant hint—wasn’t able to understand his meaning.
dn16:3.5.8: “Lord, may the Blessed One remain for an eon. May the One Well-Gone remain for an eon—for the benefit of the many, for the happiness of the many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of human beings & devas.” It was as if his mind were possessed by Māra.
dn16:3.6.1: Then the Blessed One addressed Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:3.6.3: Do what you think it is now time to do.”
dn16:3.6.4: Responding, “As you say, lord,” to the Blessed One, Ven. Ānanda got up from his seat and, bowing down to him and circumambulating him, went to sit under a tree not far from the Blessed One.
dn16:3.7.1: Then, not long after Ven. Ānanda had left, Māra the Evil One went to the Blessed One and, on arrival, stood to one side. As he was standing there, he said to the Blessed One,
dn16:3.7.2: “May the Blessed One totally unbind now, lord. May the One Well-Gone totally unbind now, lord. Now is the time for the Blessed One’s total unbinding, lord.
dn16:3.7.3: After all, these words were said by the Blessed One:
dn16:3.7.4: ‘Evil One, I will not totally unbind as long as my monk disciples are not yet experienced, trained, attained to confidence, learned, maintaining the Dhamma, practicing the Dhamma in line with the Dhamma, practicing masterfully, living in line with the Dhamma; declaring the Dhamma—after having learned it from their own teachers—teaching it, describing it, setting it forth, revealing it, explaining it, making it plain; well-refuting, in line with the Dhamma, any opposing teachings that have arisen; teaching the Dhamma with its marvels.’26
dn16:3.8.1: “But now, lord, the Blessed One’s monk disciples are experienced, trained, attained to confidence, learned, maintaining the Dhamma, practicing the Dhamma in line with the Dhamma, practicing masterfully, living in line with the Dhamma; declaring the Dhamma—after having learned it from their own teachers—teaching it, describing it, setting it forth, revealing it, explaining it, making it plain; well-refuting, in line with the Dhamma, any opposing teachings that have arisen; teaching the Dhamma with its marvels.
dn16:3.8.2: “May the Blessed One totally unbind now, lord. May the One Well-Gone totally unbind now, lord. Now is the time for the Blessed One’s total unbinding, lord.
dn16:3.8.3: After all, these words were said by the Blessed One:
dn16:3.8.4: ‘Evil One, I will not totally unbind as long as my nun disciples… my male lay-follower disciples… my female lay-follower disciples are not yet experienced, trained, attained to maturity, learned, maintaining the Dhamma, practicing the Dhamma in line with the Dhamma, practicing masterfully, living in line with the Dhamma; declaring the Dhamma—after having learned it from their own teachers—teaching it, describing it, setting it forth, revealing it, explaining it, making it plain; well-refuting, in line with the Dhamma, any opposing teachings that have arisen; teaching the Dhamma with its marvels.’
dn16:3.8.5: “But now, lord, the Blessed One’s female lay-follower disciples are experienced, trained, attained to maturity, learned, maintaining the Dhamma, practicing the Dhamma in line with the Dhamma, practicing masterfully, living in line with the Dhamma; declaring the Dhamma—after having learned it from their own teachers—teaching it, describing it, setting it forth, revealing it, explaining it, making it plain; well-refuting, in line with the Dhamma, any opposing teachings that have arisen; teaching the Dhamma with its marvels.
dn16:3.8.6: “May the Blessed One totally unbind now, lord. May the One Well-Gone totally unbind now, lord. Now is the time for the Blessed One’s total unbinding, lord.
dn16:3.8.7: After all, these words were said by the Blessed One:
dn16:3.8.8: ‘Evil One, I will not totally unbind as long as this holy life of mine is not powerful, prosperous, widely-spread, disseminated among many people, well-expounded as far as there are human beings & devas.’
dn16:3.8.9: But now, lord, the Blessed One’s holy life is powerful, prosperous, widely-spread, disseminated among many people, well-expounded as far as there are human beings & devas.
dn16:3.8.10: “May the Blessed One totally unbind now, lord. May the One Well-Gone totally unbind now, lord. Now is the time for the Blessed One’s total unbinding, lord.”
dn16:3.8.11: When this was said, the Blessed One said to Māra, the Evil One:
dn16:3.9.2: “Relax, Evil One. It won’t be long until the Tathāgata’s total unbinding.
dn16:3.9.3: In three months’ time from now, the Tathāgata will totally unbind.”
dn16:3.10.1: Thus at the Pāvāla shrine—mindful & alert—the Blessed One relinquished the fabrications of life.
dn16:3.10.2: And as the Blessed One relinquished the fabrications of life, there was a great earthquake, awesome & hair-raising, and rolls of the deva-drums split (the air).
dn16:3.10.3: Then, on realizing the significance of that, the Blessed One on that occasion exclaimed:
dn16:3.10.5: the sage relinquished the fabrication of becoming.
dn16:3.10.7: he split his own coming-into-being like a coat of mail.
dn16:3.11.1: Then the thought occurred to Ven. Ānanda:
dn16:3.11.2: “How amazing! How astounding! What a great earthquake!
dn16:3.11.3: What a very great earthquake, awesome & hair-raising, and rolls of the deva-drums split (the air)!
dn16:3.11.4: What is the reason, what is the cause, for the appearance of the great earthquake?”
dn16:3.12.1: So Ven. Ānanda went to the Blessed One and,
dn16:3.12.2: on arrival, having bowed down to him, sat to one side.
dn16:3.12.3: As he was sitting there, he said to the Blessed One,
dn16:3.12.6: What a very great earthquake, awesome & hair-raising, and rolls of thunder split (the air)!
dn16:3.12.7: What, lord, is the reason, what is the cause, for the appearance of the great earthquake?”
dn16:3.13.1: “Ānanda, there are these eight reasons, eight causes, for the appearance of a great earthquake.
dn16:3.13.3: “This great earth, Ānanda, is established on water. The water is established on wind. The wind is standing on space. There comes a time when a great wind blows. The great wind blowing shakes the water. The water, shaken, shakes the earth.
dn16:3.13.4: This is the first reason, the first cause, for the appearance of a great earthquake.
dn16:3.14.1: “Then, Ānanda, there comes a time when a brahman of power, with mastery of the mind, or a deva of great power, great might, has developed a limited earth-perception and an immeasurable liquid-perception. He makes this earth shake & shiver, quiver & quake.
dn16:3.15.1: “Then, Ānanda, when the bodhisatta, falling from the Tusita group, mindful & alert, descends into his mother’s womb, the earth shakes & shivers, quivers & quakes.
dn16:3.16.1: “Then, Ānanda, when the bodhisatta, mindful & alert, emerges from his mother’s womb, the earth shakes & shivers, quivers & quakes.
dn16:3.17.1: “Then, Ānanda, when the Tathāgata awakens to the unexcelled right self-awakening, the earth shakes & shivers, quivers & quakes.
dn16:3.18.1: “Then, Ānanda, when the Tathāgata sets rolling the unexcelled Dhamma-wheel, the earth shakes & shivers, quivers & quakes.
dn16:3.19.1: “Then, Ānanda, when the Tathāgata, mindful & alert, relinquishes the fabrications of life, the earth shakes & shivers, quivers & quakes.
dn16:3.20.1: “Then, Ānanda, when the Tathāgata, by means of the unbinding property with no fuel remaining, totally unbinds,30 the earth shakes & shivers, quivers & quakes.
dn16:3.20.2: This is the eighth reason, the eighth cause, for the appearance of a great earthquake.
dn16:3.20.3: “These are the eight reasons, the eight causes, for the appearance of a great earthquake.
dn16:3.21.3: A noble warrior assembly, a brahman assembly, a householder assembly, a contemplative assembly, a Four Great Kings assembly, a (Devas of the) Thirty-three assembly, a Māra assembly, a Brahmā assembly.
dn16:3.22.1: “I can remember approaching many hundreds of noble warrior assemblies.
dn16:3.22.2: There—before seating myself, before talking, before engaging in conversation—
dn16:3.22.3: whatever sort of appearance they had, that was the sort of appearance I had; whatever sort of accent they had, that was the sort of accent I had.
dn16:3.22.4: And I instructed, urged, roused, & encouraged them with Dhamma talk.
dn16:3.22.5: While I was speaking, they didn’t know me:
dn16:3.22.6: ‘Who is this who speaks—a deva or a human being?’
dn16:3.22.7: Having instructed, urged, roused, & encouraged them with Dhamma talk, I disappeared.
dn16:3.22.8: When I had disappeared, they didn’t know me:
dn16:3.22.9: ‘Who is this who disappeared—a deva or a human being?’
dn16:3.23.1: “I can remember approaching many hundreds of brahman assemblies…
dn16:3.23.4: many hundreds of Four Great King assemblies…
dn16:3.23.5: many hundreds of (Devas of the) Thirty-three assemblies…
dn16:3.23.7: “I can remember approaching many hundreds of Brahmā assemblies.
dn16:3.23.8: There—before seating myself, before talking, before engaging in conversation—
dn16:3.23.9: whatever sort of appearance they had, that was the sort of appearance I had;
dn16:3.23.10: whatever sort of accent they had, that was the sort of accent I had.
dn16:3.23.11: And I instructed, urged, roused, & encouraged them with Dhamma talk.
dn16:3.23.12: While I was speaking, they didn’t know me:
dn16:3.23.13: ‘Who is this person who speaks—a deva or a human being?’
dn16:3.23.14: Having instructed, urged, roused, & encouraged them with Dhamma talk, I disappeared.
dn16:3.23.15: When I had disappeared, they didn’t know me:
dn16:3.23.16: ‘Who is this who disappeared—a deva or a human being?’
dn16:3.24.1: “Ānanda, there are these eight dimensions of (mental) mastery.
dn16:3.25.1: “Having a single perception of form internally, one sees forms externally as limited, beautiful & ugly.
dn16:3.25.2: Mastering them, one has the perception, ‘I know; I see.’
dn16:3.25.3: This is the first dimension of (mental) mastery.
dn16:3.26.1: “Having a single perception of form internally, one sees forms externally as immeasurable, beautiful & ugly.
dn16:3.26.2: Mastering them, one has the perception, ‘I know; I see.’
dn16:3.26.3: This is the second dimension of (mental) mastery.
dn16:3.27.1: “Having a single formless perception internally, one sees forms externally as limited, beautiful & ugly.
dn16:3.27.2: Mastering them, one has the perception, ‘I know; I see.’
dn16:3.27.3: This is the third dimension of (mental) mastery.
dn16:3.28.1: “Having a single formless perception internally, one sees forms externally as immeasurable, beautiful & ugly.
dn16:3.28.2: Mastering them, one has the perception, ‘I know; I see.’
dn16:3.28.3: This is the fourth dimension of (mental) mastery.
dn16:3.29.1: “Having a single formless perception internally, one sees forms externally as blue, blue in their color, blue in their features, blue in their glow.
dn16:3.29.2: Just as a flax-flower is blue, blue in its color, blue in its features, blue in its glow, or just as Bārāṇasī muslin, smooth on both sides, is blue, blue in its color, blue in its features, blue in its glow;
dn16:3.29.3: in the same way, having a single formless perception internally, one sees forms externally as blue, blue in their color, blue in their features, blue in their glow.
dn16:3.29.4: Mastering them, one has the perception, ‘I know; I see.’
dn16:3.29.5: This is the fifth dimension of (mental) mastery.
dn16:3.30.1: “Having a single formless perception internally, one sees forms externally as yellow, yellow in their color, yellow in their features, yellow in their glow.
dn16:3.30.2: Just as a kaṇṇikāra flower is yellow, yellow in its color, yellow in its features, yellow in its glow, or just as Bārāṇasī muslin, smooth on both sides, is yellow, yellow in its color, yellow in its features, yellow in its glow;
dn16:3.30.3: in the same way, having a single formless perception internally, one sees forms externally as yellow, yellow in their color, yellow in their features, yellow in their glow.
dn16:3.30.4: Mastering them, one has the perception, ‘I know; I see.’
dn16:3.30.5: This is the sixth dimension of (mental) mastery.
dn16:3.31.1: “Having a single formless perception internally, one sees forms externally as red, red in their color, red in their features, red in their glow.
dn16:3.31.2: Just as a bandhu-jīvaka flower is red, red in its color, red in its features, red in its glow, or just as Bārāṇasī muslin, smooth on both sides, is red, red in its color, red in its features, red in its glow;
dn16:3.31.3: in the same way, having a single formless perception internally, one sees forms externally as red, red in their color, red in their features, red in their glow.
dn16:3.31.4: Mastering them, one has the perception, ‘I know; I see.’
dn16:3.31.5: This is the seventh dimension of (mental) mastery.
dn16:3.32.1: “Having a single formless perception internally, one sees forms externally as white, white in their color, white in their features, white in their glow.
dn16:3.32.2: Just as the morning star is white, white in its color, white in its features, white in its glow, or just as Bārāṇasī muslin, smooth on both sides, is white, white in its color, white in its features, white in its glow;
dn16:3.32.3: in the same way, having a single formless perception internally, one sees forms externally as white, white in their color, white in their features, white in their glow.
dn16:3.32.4: Mastering them, one has the perception, ‘I know; I see.’
dn16:3.32.5: This is the eighth dimension of (mental) mastery.
dn16:3.32.6: “These, Ānanda, are the eight dimensions of (mental) mastery.
dn16:3.33.5: “Not percipient of form internally, one sees forms externally.
dn16:3.33.7: “One is intent only on the beautiful.
dn16:3.33.9: “With the complete transcending of perceptions of (physical) form, with the disappearance of perceptions of resistance, and not heeding perceptions of multiplicity, (perceiving,) ‘Infinite space,’ one enters and remains in the dimension of the infinitude of space.
dn16:3.33.11: “With the complete transcending of the dimension of the infinitude of space, (perceiving,) ‘Infinite consciousness,’ one enters and remains in the dimension of the infinitude of consciousness.
dn16:3.33.13: “With the complete transcending of the dimension of the infinitude of consciousness, (perceiving,) ‘There is nothing,’ one enters and remains in the dimension of nothingness.
dn16:3.33.15: “With the complete transcending of the dimension of nothingness, one enters and remains in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception.
dn16:3.33.17: “With the complete transcending of the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, one enters and remains in the cessation of perception and feeling.
dn16:3.34.1: “One time, Ānanda, I was staying in Uruvelā on the bank of the Nerañjarā River at the Goat-herd Banyan, newly awakened.
dn16:3.34.2: Then Māra, the Evil One, approached me and, on arrival, stood to one side. As he was standing there, he said to me, ‘May the Blessed One totally unbind now, lord.
dn16:3.34.3: May the One Well-Gone totally unbind now, lord. Now is the time for the Blessed One’s total unbinding, lord.’
dn16:3.34.4: “When this was said, I said to Māra, the Evil One,
dn16:3.35.1: ‘Evil One, I will not totally unbind as long as my monk disciples are not yet experienced, trained, attained to confidence, learned, maintaining the Dhamma, practicing the Dhamma in line with the Dhamma, practicing masterfully, living in line with the Dhamma; declaring the Dhamma—after having learned it from their own teachers—teaching it, describing it, setting it forth, revealing it, explaining it, making it plain; well-refuting, in line with the Dhamma, any opposing teachings that have arisen; teaching the Dhamma with its marvels.’
dn16:3.35.2: “‘Evil One, I will not totally unbind as long as my nun disciples…
dn16:3.35.4: my female lay-follower disciples are not yet experienced, trained, attained to maturity, learned, maintaining the Dhamma, practicing the Dhamma in line with the Dhamma, practicing masterfully, living in line with the Dhamma; declaring the Dhamma—after having learned it from their own teachers—teaching it, describing it, setting it forth, revealing it, explaining it, making it plain; well-refuting, in line with the Dhamma, any opposing teachings that have arisen; teaching the Dhamma with its marvels.
dn16:3.35.5: “‘Evil One, I will not totally unbind as long as this holy life of mine is not powerful, prosperous, widely-spread, disseminated among many people, well-expounded as far as there are human beings & devas.’
dn16:3.36.1: “And just now, Ānanda, (here) at the Pāvāla Shrine, Māra the Evil One approached me and, on arrival, stood to one side. As he was standing here, he said to me,
dn16:3.36.2: ‘May the Blessed One totally unbind now, lord. May the One Well-Gone totally unbind now, lord. Now is the time for the Blessed One’s total unbinding, lord.
dn16:3.36.3: After all, these words were said by the Blessed One:
dn16:3.36.4: “Evil One, I will not totally unbind as long as my monk disciples are not yet experienced,
dn16:3.36.5: as long as my nun disciples…
dn16:3.36.7: my female lay-follower disciples are not yet experienced…
dn16:3.36.8: as long as this holy life of mine is not powerful, prosperous, widely-spread, disseminated among many people, well-expounded as far as there are human beings & devas.”
dn16:3.36.9: But now, lord, the Blessed One’s holy life is powerful, prosperous, widely-spread, disseminated among many people, well-expounded as far as there are human beings & devas.
dn16:3.36.10: “‘May the Blessed One totally unbind now, lord. May the One Well-Gone totally unbind now, lord. Now is the time for the Blessed One’s total unbinding, lord.’
dn16:3.37.1: “When this was said, I said to Māra, the Evil One:
dn16:3.37.2: ‘Relax, Evil One. It won’t be long until the Tathāgata’s total unbinding.
dn16:3.37.3: In three months’ time from now, the Tathāgata will totally unbind.’
dn16:3.37.4: “Just now, Ānanda, here at the Pāvāla shrine—mindful & alert—I relinquished the fabrications of life.”
dn16:3.38.1: When this was said, Ven. Ānanda said to the Blessed One,
dn16:3.38.2: “Lord, may the Blessed One remain for an eon. May the One Well-Gone remain for an eon—for the benefit of the many, for the happiness of the many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of human beings & devas.”
dn16:3.38.4: Don’t beg the Tathāgata. Now is not the time for begging the Tathāgata.”
dn16:3.39.2: A third time, Ven. Ānanda said to the Blessed One, “Lord, may the Blessed One remain for an eon.
dn16:3.39.3: May the One Well-Gone remain for an eon—for the benefit of the many, for the happiness of the many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of human beings & devas.”
dn16:3.39.4: “Ānanda, do you have conviction in the Tathāgata’s awakening?”
dn16:3.39.6: “Then why, Ānanda, do you harass the Tathāgata up to three times?”
dn16:3.40.1: “Face-to-face with the Blessed One have I heard this, face-to-face have I received this:
dn16:3.40.2: ‘Anyone, Ānanda, in whom the four bases of power are developed, pursued, given a means of transport, given a grounding, steadied, consolidated, and well-undertaken, could—if he wanted—remain for an eon or the remainder of an eon.
dn16:3.40.3: In the Tathāgata, Ānanda, the four bases of power are developed, pursued, given a means of transport, given a grounding, steadied, consolidated, and well-undertaken. He could—if he wanted—remain for an eon or the remainder of an eon.’”
dn16:3.40.4: “Ānanda, do you have conviction in the Tathāgata’s awakening?”
dn16:3.40.6: “Then the wrong-doing is yours, Ānanda. Yours the mistake, in that—even when the Tathāgata had given such a blatant sign, such a blatant hint—you weren’t able to understand his meaning. You didn’t beg of the Tathāgata,
dn16:3.40.7: ‘Lord, may the Blessed One remain for an eon. May the One Well-Gone remain for an eon—for the benefit of the many, for the happiness of the many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of human beings & devas.’
dn16:3.40.8: If you had begged the Tathāgata two times, the Tathāgata would have refused, but after the third time he would have acquiesced.
dn16:3.40.9: The wrong-doing is yours, Ānanda. Yours the mistake.

dn16:3.42.5: right there near Rājagaha under the rock overhang at Serpents Water Hole in the Cool Forest…
