dn16:3.42.7: right there near Rājagaha at the Bamboo Forest, the Squirrels’ Feeding Ground…
dn16:3.42.8: right there near Rājagaha at Jīvaka’s Mango Grove…
dn16:3.42.9: right there near Rājagaha at the Maddakucchi Deer Park.
dn16:3.43.2: ‘Rājagaha is refreshing, Ānanda. Refreshing, too, are Vulture Peak Mountain, the Gotama Banyan tree, Robbers Cliff, Sattapaṇṇa Cave, the Black Rock on Isigili Mountain, the rock overhang at Serpents Water Hole in the Cool Forest, Tapodā Park, the Bamboo Forest, the Squirrels’ Feeding Ground, Jīvaka’s Mango Grove, the Maddakucchi Deer Park.
dn16:3.44.1: Anyone, Ānanda, in whom the four bases of power are developed, pursued, given a means of transport, given a grounding, steadied, consolidated, and well-undertaken, could—if he wanted—remain for an eon or the remainder of an eon. In the Tathāgata, Ānanda, the four bases of power are developed, pursued, given a means of transport, given a grounding, steadied, consolidated, and well-undertaken.
dn16:3.44.2: He could—if he wanted—remain for an eon or the remainder of an eon.’
dn16:3.44.3: But you, Ānanda—even when the Tathāgata had given such a blatant sign, such a blatant hint—weren’t able to understand his meaning. You didn’t beg of the Tathāgata,
dn16:3.44.4: ‘Lord, may the Blessed One remain for an eon. May the One Well-Gone remain for an eon—for the benefit of the many, for the happiness of the many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of human beings & devas.’
dn16:3.44.5: If you had begged the Tathāgata two times, the Tathāgata would have refused, but after the third time he would have acquiesced.
dn16:3.44.6: The wrong-doing is yours, Ānanda. Yours the mistake.
dn16:3.45.1: “On one occasion, Ānanda, I was staying right here in Vesālī at the Udena shrine…
dn16:3.47.1: “And just now, Ānanda, (here) at Pāvāla Shrine, I addressed you today:
dn16:3.47.2: ‘Vesālī is refreshing, Ānanda. Refreshing, too, are the Udena shrine, the Gotamaka shrine, the Sattamba shrine, the ManySon shrine, the Sāranda shrine, the Pāvāla shrine.
dn16:3.47.3: Anyone, Ānanda, in whom the four bases of power are developed, pursued, given a means of transport, given a grounding, steadied, consolidated, and well-undertaken, could—if he wanted—remain for an eon or the remainder of an eon.
dn16:3.47.4: In the Tathāgata, Ānanda, the four bases of power are developed, pursued, given a means of transport, given a grounding, steadied, consolidated, and well-undertaken. He could—if he wanted—remain for an eon or the remainder of an eon.’
dn16:3.47.5: But you, Ānanda—even when the Tathāgata had given such a blatant sign, such a blatant hint—weren’t able to understand his meaning. You didn’t beg of the Tathāgata,
dn16:3.47.6: ‘Lord, may the Blessed One remain for an eon. May the One Well-Gone remain for an eon—for the benefit of the many, for the happiness of the many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of human beings & devas.’
dn16:3.47.7: If you had begged the Tathāgata two times, the Tathāgata would have refused, but after the third time he would have acquiesced.
dn16:3.47.8: The wrong-doing is yours, Ānanda. Yours the mistake.
dn16:3.48.2: different-becoming, separate-becoming, otherwise-becoming of all that is dear & appealing?
dn16:3.48.3: What else is there to expect? That of anything born, become, fabricated, subject to disintegration, you might say, ‘O, may it not disintegrate’? The possibility doesn’t exist.
dn16:3.48.4: And that is what the Tathāgata has gotten rid of, vomited up, released, abandoned, forfeited. It was with the fabrications of life relinquished that this categorical statement was spoken:
dn16:3.48.5: ‘It won’t be long until the Tathāgata’s total unbinding.
dn16:3.48.6: In three months’ time from now, the Tathāgata will totally unbind.’
dn16:3.48.7: For the Tathāgata, for the sake of life, to rescind that: The possibility doesn’t exist.
dn16:3.48.8: “Come, now, Ānanda. We will head for the Gabled Hall in the Great Forest.”
dn16:3.48.9: “As you say, lord,” Ven. Ānanda responded to the Blessed One.
dn16:3.49.1: Then the Blessed One together with Ven. Ānanda went to the Great Forest and to the Gabled Hall. On arrival, the Blessed One said to Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:3.49.2: “Go, Ānanda. Have all the monks living in dependence on Vesālī gather at the assembly hall.”
dn16:3.49.3: Responding, “As you say, lord,” to the Blessed One, Ven. Ānanda—having had all the monks living in dependence on Vesālī gather at the assembly hall—approached the Blessed One. On arrival, having bowed down to him, he stood to one side. As he was standing there, he said to the Blessed One,
dn16:3.49.4: “The Saṅgha of monks has gathered, lord. May the Blessed One do what he thinks it is now time to do.”
dn16:3.50.1: Then the Blessed One went to the assembly hall and, on arrival, sat down on the seat laid out.
dn16:3.50.2: Having sat down, he addressed the monks:
dn16:3.50.3: “Monks, the qualities I have taught, having known them directly: You should grasp them thoroughly, cultivate them, develop them, & pursue them so that this holy life may long endure & remain steadfast for the benefit, welfare, & happiness of the multitude, out of sympathy for the world, for the benefit, welfare, & happiness of human beings & devas. And which are the qualities I have taught…
dn16:3.50.4: for the benefit, welfare, & happiness of human beings & devas?
dn16:3.50.5: The four establishings of mindfulness, the four right exertions, the four bases of power, the five faculties, the five strengths, the seven factors for awakening, the noble eightfold path.
dn16:3.51.1: These are the qualities I have taught, having known them directly, that you should grasp thoroughly, cultivate, develop, & pursue so that this holy life may long endure & remain steadfast for the benefit, welfare, & happiness of the multitude, out of sympathy for the world, for the benefit, welfare, & happiness of human beings & devas.”
dn16:3.51.2: Then the Blessed One addressed the monks,
dn16:3.51.4: All fabrications are subject to decay. Reach consummation through heedfulness.
dn16:3.51.5: It won’t be long until the Tathāgata’s total unbinding.
dn16:3.51.6: In three months’ time from now, the Tathāgata will totally unbind.”
dn16:3.51.7: That is what the Blessed One said.
dn16:3.51.8: Having said that, the One Well-Gone said further:
dn16:3.51.9: Young & old wise & foolish rich & poor: all end up dying.
dn16:3.51.10: As a potter’s clay vessels large & small fired & unfired
dn16:3.51.12: so too life heads to death. Then the Teacher said further: Ripe my age, little the life remaining to me. Leaving you, I will go, having made my refuge for myself.
dn16:3.51.17: He who, in this Dhamma & Vinaya,
dn16:3.51.20: through birth, will make an end of stress.
dn16:4.1.1: Then the Blessed One, early in the morning, adjusted his lower robe and—taking his bowl & outer robe—went into Vesālī for alms.
dn16:4.1.2: Having gone for alms in Vesālī, after the meal, returning from his alms round, gazing on Vesālī with an elephant’s look, he addressed Ven. Ānanda:
dn16:4.1.3: “Ānanda, this is the Tathāgata’s last look at Vesālī.
dn16:4.1.4: Come, Ānanda, we will head for Bhaṇḍa Village.
dn16:4.1.5: “As you say, lord,” Ven. Ānanda responded to the Blessed One.
dn16:4.2.1: Then the Blessed One, together with a large Saṅgha of monks, went to Bhaṇḍa Village.
dn16:4.2.2: There the Blessed One stayed near Bhaṇḍa Village.
dn16:4.2.3: And there he addressed the monks,
dn16:4.2.4: “It’s through not awakening to or penetrating four qualities, monks, that we have transmigrated and wandered on for such a long time, you & I.
dn16:4.2.6: “It’s through not awakening to or penetrating noble virtue that we have transmigrated and wandered on for such a long time, you & I.
dn16:4.2.7: It’s through not awakening to or penetrating noble concentration…
dn16:4.2.8: It’s through not awakening to or penetrating noble discernment…
dn16:4.2.9: It’s through not awakening to or penetrating noble release that we have transmigrated and wandered on for such a long time, you & I.
dn16:4.2.10: “(But now,) this noble virtue has been awakened to & penetrated, noble concentration has been awakened to & penetrated, noble discernment has been awakened to & penetrated, noble release has been awakened to & penetrated. Craving for becoming has been crushed, the guide to becoming [i.e., craving] is ended. There now is no further-becoming.”
dn16:4.3.1: That is what the Blessed One said.
dn16:4.3.2: Having said it, the Teacher, the One Well-Gone, said further:
dn16:4.3.5: These qualities have been awakened to
dn16:4.3.7: Directly knowing this, he shows it to the monks—
dn16:4.3.9: the teacher who has made an ending of stress,
dn16:4.3.10: the one with eyes, totally unbound.
dn16:4.4.1: While staying there at Bhaṇḍa Village, the Blessed One often gave this Dhamma talk to the monks:
dn16:4.4.2: “Such is virtue, such is concentration, such is discernment.
dn16:4.4.3: Concentration nurtured with virtue is of great fruit, great reward.
dn16:4.4.4: Discernment nurtured with concentration is of great fruit, great reward.
dn16:4.4.5: The mind nurtured with discernment is rightly released from the effluents, i.e.,
dn16:4.4.6: the effluent of sensuality, the effluent of becoming, the effluent of ignorance.”
dn16:4.5.1: Then the Blessed One, having stayed near Bhaṇḍa Village as long as he pleased, said to Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:4.5.2: “Come, Ānanda, we will head for Hatthi Village,
dn16:4.6.1: “As you say, lord,” Ven. Ānanda responded to the Blessed One.
dn16:4.6.2: Then the Blessed One, together with a large Saṅgha of monks, went to Bhoganagara.
dn16:4.7.1: There the Blessed One stayed near Bhoganagara at the Ānanda shrine.
dn16:4.7.2: There the Blessed One addressed the monks,
dn16:4.7.3: “Monks, I will teach you four great standards.
dn16:4.7.5: “As you say, lord,” the monks responded to him.
dn16:4.8.1: “There is the case where a monk says this:
dn16:4.8.2: ‘Face-to-face with the Blessed One have I heard this, face-to-face have I received this:
dn16:4.8.3: This is the Dhamma, this is the Vinaya, this is the Teacher’s instruction.’
dn16:4.8.4: His statement is neither to be approved nor scorned.
dn16:4.8.5: Without approval or scorn, take careful note of his words and make them stand against the suttas and tally them against the Vinaya.
dn16:4.8.6: If, on making them stand against the suttas and tallying them against the Vinaya, you find that they don’t stand with the suttas or tally with the Vinaya, you may conclude:
dn16:4.8.7: ‘This is not the word of the Blessed One;
dn16:4.8.10: But if, on making them stand against the suttas and tallying them against the Vinaya, you find that they stand with the suttas and tally with the Vinaya, you may conclude:
dn16:4.8.11: ‘This is the word of the Blessed One;
dn16:4.8.12: this monk has understood it rightly.’
dn16:4.9.1: “Then there is the case where a monk says this:
dn16:4.9.2: ‘In a monastery over there dwells a Saṅgha with well-known leading elders.
dn16:4.9.3: Face-to-face with that Saṅgha I have heard this, face-to-face have I received this:
dn16:4.9.4: This is the Dhamma, this is the Vinaya, this is the Teacher’s instruction.’ …
dn16:4.10.1: “Then there is the case where a monk says this:
dn16:4.10.2: ‘In a monastery over there dwell many learned elder monks, well-versed in the tradition, who have memorized the Dhamma, the Vinaya, and the Mātikā.
dn16:4.10.3: Face-to-face with those elders I have heard this, face-to-face have I received this:
dn16:4.10.4: This is the Dhamma, this is the Vinaya, this is the Teacher’s instruction.’ …
dn16:4.11.1: “Then there is the case where a monk says this:
dn16:4.11.2: ‘In a monastery over there dwells a learned elder monk, well-versed in the tradition, who has memorized the Dhamma, the Vinaya, and the Mātikā.
dn16:4.11.3: Face-to-face with that elder I have heard this, face-to-face have I received this:
dn16:4.11.4: This is the Dhamma, this is the Vinaya, this is the Teacher’s instruction.’
dn16:4.11.5: His statement is neither to be approved nor scorned.
dn16:4.11.6: Without approval or scorn, take careful note of his words and make them stand against the suttas and tally them against the Vinaya.

dn16:4.11.12: ‘This is the word of the Blessed One;

dn16:4.12.1: While staying there near Bhoganagara at the Ānanda shrine, the Blessed One often gave this Dhamma talk to the monks:
dn16:4.12.2: “Such is virtue, such is concentration, such is discernment.
dn16:4.12.3: Concentration nurtured with virtue is of great fruit, great reward.
dn16:4.12.4: Discernment nurtured with concentration is of great fruit, great reward.
