dn16:4.13.1: Then the Blessed One, having stayed near Bhoganagara at the Ānanda shrine as long as he pleased, said to Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:4.13.2: “Come, Ānanda, we will head for Pāvā.”
dn16:4.13.3: “As you say, lord,” Ven. Ānanda responded to the Blessed One.
dn16:4.13.4: Then the Blessed One, together with a large Saṅgha of monks, went to Pāvā.
dn16:4.13.5: There the Blessed One stayed near Pāvā in the mango grove of Cunda the silversmith.
dn16:4.14.1: Cunda the silversmith heard, “The Blessed One, they say, on reaching Pāvā, is staying near Pāvā in my mango grove.”
dn16:4.14.2: So Cunda went to the Blessed One and, on arrival, having bowed down to him, sat to one side.
dn16:4.14.3: As he was sitting there, the Blessed One instructed, urged, roused, & encouraged him with Dhamma talk.
dn16:4.14.4: Then Cunda—instructed, urged, roused, & encouraged by the Blessed One’s Dhamma talk—said to him,
dn16:4.15.1: “Lord, may the Blessed One acquiesce to my meal tomorrow, together with the Saṅgha of monks.”
dn16:4.15.2: The Blessed One acquiesced with silence.
dn16:4.16.1: Then Cunda, understanding the Blessed One’s acquiescence, got up from his seat and, bowing down to him and circumambulating him, left.
dn16:4.17.1: Then, at the end of the night, after having exquisite staple & non-staple food—including a large amount of pig-delicacy36—prepared in his own home, he announced the time to the Blessed One:
dn16:4.17.2: “It’s time, lord. The meal is ready.”
dn16:4.18.1: Then the Blessed One, early in the morning, adjusted his lower robe and—taking his bowl & outer robe—went together with the Saṅgha of monks to Cunda’s home. On arrival, he sat down on the seat laid out.
dn16:4.18.3: “Cunda, serve me with the pig-delicacy you have had prepared,
dn16:4.18.4: and the Saṅgha of monks with the other staple & non-staple food you have had prepared.”
dn16:4.18.5: Responding, “As you say, lord,” to the Blessed One, Cunda served the Blessed One with the pig-delicacy he had had prepared,
dn16:4.18.6: and the Saṅgha of monks with the other staple & non-staple food he had had prepared.
dn16:4.19.1: Then the Blessed One said to him,
dn16:4.19.2: “Cunda, bury the remaining pig-delicacy in a pit.
dn16:4.19.3: I don’t see anyone in the world together with its devas, Māras, &Brahmās, in this generation with its contemplatives &brahmans, its rulers &commonfolk, in whom, when it was ingested, it would go to a healthy change, aside from the Tathāgata.”
dn16:4.19.4: Responding, “As you say, lord,” to the Blessed One, Cunda buried the remaining pig-delicacy in a pit, went to the Blessed One and, on arrival, having bowed down to him, sat to one side.
dn16:4.19.5: As he was sitting there, the Blessed One—after instructing, urging, rousing, & encouraging him with Dhamma talk—got up from his seat and left.
dn16:4.20.1: Then in the Blessed One, after he had eaten Cunda’s meal, there arose a severe disease accompanied with (the passing of) blood, with intense pains & deadly.
dn16:4.20.2: But the Blessed One endured it—mindful, alert, & not struck down by it.
dn16:4.20.5: “As you say, lord,” Ven. Ānanda responded to the Blessed One.
dn16:4.20.7: on eating Cunda the silversmith’s meal,
dn16:4.20.8: the enlightened one was touched by illness—
dn16:4.20.10: After he had eaten the pig-delicacy,
dn16:4.20.11: a fierce sickness arose in the Teacher.
dn16:4.20.12: After being purged of it, the Blessed One said,
dn16:4.20.13: “To the city of Kusinārā I will go.”
dn16:4.21.1: Then the Blessed One, going down from the road, went to a certain tree and, on arrival, said to Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:4.21.2: “Ānanda, please arrange my outer robe folded in four. I am tired. I will sit down.”
dn16:4.21.3: Responding, “As you say, lord,” to the Blessed One, Ven. Ānanda arranged the outer robe folded in four.
dn16:4.21.4: The Blessed One sat down on the seat laid out.
dn16:4.22.1: Seated, he said to Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:4.22.2: “Ānanda, please fetch me some water. I am thirsty. I will drink.”
dn16:4.22.3: When this was said, Ven. Ānanda said to the Blessed One,
dn16:4.22.4: “Lord, just now 500 carts have passed through. The meager river—cut by the wheels—flows turbid & disturbed.
dn16:4.22.5: But the Kakudha river is not far away, with pristine water, pleasing water, cool water, pellucid water,38 with restful banks, refreshing.
dn16:4.22.6: There the Blessed One will drink potable water and cool his limbs.”
dn16:4.23.1: A second time, the Blessed One said to Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:4.23.2: “Ānanda, please fetch me some water. I am thirsty. I will drink.”
dn16:4.23.3: A second time, Ven. Ānanda said to the Blessed One,
dn16:4.23.4: “Lord, just now 500 carts have passed through. The meager water—cut by the wheels—flows turbid & disturbed.
dn16:4.23.5: But the Kakudha River is not far away, with pristine water, pleasing water, cool water, pellucid water, with restful banks, refreshing.
dn16:4.23.6: There the Blessed One will drink potable water and cool his limbs.”
dn16:4.24.1: A third time, the Blessed One said to Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:4.24.2: “Ānanda, please fetch me some water. I am thirsty. I will drink.”
dn16:4.24.3: Responding, “As you say, lord,” to the Blessed One, Ven. Ānanda—taking a bowl—went to the river.
dn16:4.24.4: And the meager river that, cut by the wheels, had been flowing turbid & disturbed, on his approach flowed pristine, clear, & undisturbed.
dn16:4.25.2: “How amazing! How astounding!—the great power & great might of the Tathāgata!—
dn16:4.25.3: in that this meager river that, cut by the wheels, was flowing turbid & disturbed, on my approach flowed pristine, clear, & undisturbed!”
dn16:4.25.4: Fetching water with the bowl, he went to the Blessed One and on arrival said,
dn16:4.25.5: “How amazing! How astounding!—the great power & great might of the Tathāgata!—
dn16:4.25.6: in that this meager river that, cut by the wheels, was flowing turbid & disturbed, on my approach flowed pristine, clear, & undisturbed!
dn16:4.25.7: Drink the water, O Blessed One! Drink the water, O One Well-Gone!”
dn16:4.25.8: Then the Blessed One drank the water.
dn16:4.26.1: Now on that occasion Pukkusa Mallaputta, a disciple of Āḷāra Kālāma was traveling on the road from Kusinārā to Pāvā.
dn16:4.26.2: He saw the Blessed One sitting at the root of a tree and, on seeing him, approached him.
dn16:4.26.3: On arrival, having bowed down to him, he sat to one side. As he was sitting there, he said to the Blessed One,
dn16:4.26.4: “It’s amazing, lord. It’s astounding: the peaceful abiding by which those gone forth abide.
dn16:4.27.1: Once, Āḷāra Kālāma, while traveling, went down from the road and sat not far away at the root of a tree for the day’s abiding.
dn16:4.27.2: Then 500 carts passed by right near him.
dn16:4.27.3: Then a certain man, coming along right behind those hundreds of carts, approached Āḷāra Kālāma and, on arrival, said to him,
dn16:4.27.4: ‘Venerable sir, did you see 500 carts going past?’
dn16:4.27.12: ‘Then, venerable sir, being conscious & awake when 500 carts passed by right near you, you neither saw them nor heard a sound.
dn16:4.27.13: And yet your outer robe is covered with (their) dust!’
dn16:4.27.15: Then the thought occurred to that man,
dn16:4.27.16: ‘How amazing! How astounding: the peaceful abiding by which those gone forth abide—
dn16:4.27.17: in that, being conscious & awake when 500 carts passed by right near, he would neither see them nor hear a sound!’
dn16:4.27.18: Having proclaimed immense conviction in Āḷāra Kālāma, he then left.”
dn16:4.28.2: Which is more difficult to do, more difficult to master:
dn16:4.28.3: one who, being conscious & awake when 500 carts passed by right near, would neither see them nor hear a sound;
dn16:4.28.4: or one who, being conscious & awake when the rain-deva was raining, the rain-deva was pouring, lightning-streaks were shooting out, and a thunderbolt split (the air), would neither see them nor hear a sound?”
dn16:4.29.1: “Lord, what would 500 carts amount to—or 600 carts, or 700 carts, or 800 carts, or 900 carts, or 1,000 carts… or 100,000 carts?
dn16:4.29.2: That would be more difficult to do, more difficult to master: one who, being conscious & awake when the rain-deva was raining, the rain-deva was pouring, lightning-streaks were shooting out, and a thunderbolt split (the air), would neither see them nor hear a sound.”
dn16:4.30.1: “One time, Pukkusa, I was staying near Ātumā in a threshing barn.
dn16:4.30.2: And on that occasion, when the rain-deva was raining, the rain-deva was pouring, lightning-streaks were shooting out, and a thunderbolt split (the air) not far from the threshing barn, two farmers—brothers—were killed, along with four oxen.
dn16:4.30.3: “Then a large crowd of people came out of Ātumā to where the two farmers—brothers—were killed, along with the four oxen.
dn16:4.31.1: And on that occasion I, having come out of the threshing barn, was doing walking meditation in front of the door to the threshing barn.
dn16:4.31.2: A certain man from the great crowd of people approached me and, on arrival, having bowed down to me, stood to one side. As he was standing there, I said to him,
dn16:4.32.1: ‘Why, friend, has this great crowd of people gathered?’
dn16:4.32.2: “‘Just now, lord—when the rain-deva was raining, the rain-deva was pouring, lightning-streaks were shooting out, and a thunderbolt split (the air)—two farmers—brothers—were killed, along with four oxen.
dn16:4.32.3: That’s why this great crowd of people has gathered.
dn16:4.32.4: But you, lord: Where were you?’
dn16:4.32.14: “‘Then, lord, being conscious & awake when the rain-deva was raining, the rain-deva was pouring, lightning-streaks were shooting out, and a thunderbolt split (the air), you neither saw anything nor heard a sound.’
dn16:4.33.1: “Then the thought occurred to that man,
dn16:4.33.2: ‘How amazing! How astounding: the peaceful abiding by which those gone forth abide—
dn16:4.33.3: in that, when the rain-deva was raining, the rain-deva was pouring, lightning-streaks were shooting out, and a thunderbolt split (the air), he would neither see them nor hear a sound!’
dn16:4.33.4: Having proclaimed immense conviction in me, he circumambulated me and then left.”
dn16:4.34.1: When this was said, Pukkusa Mallaputta said to the Blessed One,
dn16:4.34.2: “The conviction I had in Āḷāra Kālāma I winnow before a high wind or wash away in the swift current of a river.
dn16:4.34.4: Just as if he were to place upright what was overturned, to reveal what was hidden, to show the way to one who was lost, or to carry a lamp into the dark so that those with eyes could see forms, in the same way has the Blessed One—through many lines of reasoning—made the Dhamma clear.
dn16:4.34.5: I go to the Blessed One for refuge, to the Dhamma, and to the Saṅgha of monks.
dn16:4.34.6: May the Blessed One remember me as a lay follower who has gone to him for refuge, from this day forward, for life.”
dn16:4.35.1: Then Pukkusa Mallaputta addressed a certain man,
dn16:4.35.2: “Come now, I say. Fetch me a pair of gold-colored robes, ready to wear.”
dn16:4.35.3: Responding, “As you say, lord,” to Pukkusa Mallaputta, the man fetched the pair of gold-colored robes, ready to wear.
dn16:4.35.4: Then Pukkusa Mallaputta presented the pair of gold-colored robes, ready to wear, to the Blessed One, (saying,)
dn16:4.35.5: “May the Blessed One accept from me this pair of gold-colored robes, ready to wear, out of kindness.”
dn16:4.35.6: “Very well, then, Pukkusa. Clothe me with one, and Ānanda with the other.”
dn16:4.35.7: Responding, “As you say, lord,” to the Blessed One, Pukkusa Mallaputta clothed the Blessed One with one, and Ven. Ānanda with the other.
dn16:4.36.1: Then the Blessed One instructed, urged, roused, and encouraged Pukkusa Mallaputta with Dhamma talk.
dn16:4.36.2: Then Pukkusa Mallaputta—instructed, urged, roused, and encouraged with the Blessed One’s Dhamma talk—got up from his seat and, bowing down to the Blessed One and circumambulating him, left.
dn16:4.37.1: Then not long after Pukkusa Mallaputta had left, Ven. Ānanda placed the pair of gold-colored robes, ready to wear, against the Blessed One’s body.
dn16:4.37.2: Placed against the Blessed One’s body, their iridescence seemed as if destroyed.
dn16:4.37.4: “It’s amazing, lord. It’s astounding—how clear & bright the color of the Tathāgata’s skin!
dn16:4.37.5: When this pair of gold-colored robes, ready to wear, is placed against the Blessed One’s body, their iridescence seems as if destroyed!”
dn16:4.37.6: “So it is, Ānanda. There are two times when the color of the Tathāgata’s skin is especially clear & bright.
dn16:4.38.2: The night on which the Tathāgata awakens to the unexcelled self-awakening, and the night on which the Tathāgata totally unbinds by means of the unbinding property with no fuel remaining.
dn16:4.38.3: These are the two times when the color of the Tathāgata’s skin is especially clear & bright.
dn16:4.38.4: Today, in the last watch of the night, between the twin Sal trees in Upavattana, the Mallans’ Sal Forest near Kusinārā, the Tathāgata’s total unbinding will occur.
dn16:4.38.5: “Come, Ānanda, we will head for Kakudha River.”
dn16:4.38.6: “As you say, lord,” Ven. Ānanda responded to the Blessed One.
dn16:4.38.7: Pukkusa offered a pair of gold-colored robes
dn16:4.39.1: Then the Blessed One, together with the large Saṅgha of monks, went to the Kakudha River and, after arriving at the Kakudha River, going down, bathing, drinking, & coming back out, went to a mango grove.
dn16:4.39.2: On arrival, the Blessed One said to Ven. Cundaka,
dn16:4.39.3: “Cundaka, please arrange my outer robe folded in four. I am tired. I will lie down.”
dn16:4.40.1: Responding, “As you say, lord,” to the Blessed One, Ven. Cundaka arranged the outer robe folded in four.
dn16:4.40.2: The Blessed One, lying on his right side, took up the lion’s posture, placing one foot on top of the other—mindful, alert, and attending to the perception of getting up.
dn16:4.40.3: Ven. Cundaka sat in front of him.
dn16:4.41.1: The awakened one, —having gone to the little Kakudha river
dn16:4.41.2: with its pristine, pleasing water, clear—
dn16:4.41.4: the Tathāgata, unequalled in the world
dn16:4.41.5: went down, bathed, drank, & came out.
dn16:4.41.6: Honored, surrounded, in the midst of the group of monks,
dn16:4.41.7: the Blessed One, Teacher, proceeding here in the Dhamma,
dn16:4.41.8: the great seer, went to the mango grove.
dn16:4.41.9: He addressed the monk named Cundaka,
dn16:4.41.10: “Spread it out, folded in four for me to lie down.”
dn16:4.41.11: Ordered by the One of developed mind,
dn16:4.41.12: Cundaka quickly set it out, folded in four.
dn16:4.41.13: The Teacher lay down, seeming very tired,
dn16:4.41.14: and Cundaka sat down there before him.
dn16:4.42.1: Then the Blessed One addressed Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:4.42.2: “Ānanda, if anyone tries to incite remorse in Cunda the silversmith, saying,
dn16:4.42.3: ‘It’s no gain for you, friend Cunda, it’s ill-done by you, that the Tathāgata, having eaten your last alms, totally unbound,’
dn16:4.42.4: then Cunda’s remorse should be allayed (in this way):
dn16:4.42.5: ‘It’s a gain for you, friend Cunda, it’s well-done by you,
dn16:4.42.6: that the Tathāgata, having eaten your last alms, totally unbound.
dn16:4.42.7: Face to face with the Blessed One have I heard it, face to face have I learned it,
dn16:4.42.8: “These two alms are equal to each other in fruit, equal to each other in result, of much greater fruit & reward than any other alms.
dn16:4.42.10: The alms that, after having eaten it, the Tathāgata awakens to the unexcelled right self-awakening. And the alms that, after having eaten it, the Tathāgata unbinds by means of the unbinding property with no fuel remaining.
dn16:4.42.11: These are the two alms that are equal to each other in fruit, equal to each other in result, of much greater fruit & reward than any other alms.
dn16:4.42.12: Venerable Cunda the silversmith has accumulated kamma that leads to long life. Venerable Cunda the silversmith has accumulated kamma that leads to beauty. Venerable Cunda the silversmith has accumulated kamma that leads to happiness. Venerable Cunda the silversmith has accumulated kamma that leads to heaven. Venerable Cunda the silversmith has accumulated kamma that leads to rank. Venerable Cunda the silversmith has accumulated kamma that leads to sovereignty.”’
dn16:4.42.13: In this way, Ānanda, Cunda the silversmith’s remorse should be allayed.”
dn16:4.43.1: Then, on realizing the significance of that, the Blessed One on that occasion exclaimed:
dn16:4.43.2: For a person giving, merit increases.
dn16:4.43.3: For one self-restraining, no animosity is amassed.
dn16:4.43.4: One who is skillful leaves evil behind and
dn16:4.43.5: —from the ending of passion, aversion, delusion—totally unbinds.
dn16:5.1.1: Then the Blessed One said to Ven. Ānanda:
dn16:5.1.2: “Come, Ānanda, we will head for the far shore of the Hiraññavati River, for Upavattana, the Mallans’ Sal Forest near Kusinārā.”
dn16:5.1.3: “As you say, lord,” Ven. Ānanda responded to the Blessed One.
dn16:5.1.4: Then the Blessed One with a large Saṅgha of monks went to the far shore of the Hiraññavati River, to Upavattana, the Mallans’ Sal Forest near Kusinārā. On arrival, he said to Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:5.1.5: “Ānanda, please prepare a bed for me between the twin Sal trees, with its head to the north. I am tired and will lie down.”
dn16:5.1.6: Responding, “As you say, lord,” to the Blessed One, Ven. Ānanda prepared a bed between the twin Sal trees, with its head to the north.
dn16:5.1.7: Then the Blessed One lay down on his right side in the lion’s sleeping posture, with one foot on top of the other, mindful & alert.
dn16:5.2.1: Now on that occasion the twin Sal trees were in full bloom, even though it was not the time for flowering.
dn16:5.2.2: They showered, strewed, & sprinkled on the Tathāgata’s body in homage to him.
dn16:5.2.3: Heavenly coral-tree blossoms fell from the sky, showering, strewing, & sprinkling the Tathāgata’s body in homage to him.
dn16:5.2.4: Heavenly sandalwood powder fell from the sky, showering, strewing, & sprinkling the Tathāgata’s body in homage to him.
dn16:5.2.5: Heavenly music was playing in the sky, in homage to the Tathāgata.
dn16:5.2.6: Heavenly songs were sung in the sky, in homage to the Tathāgata.
dn16:5.3.1: Then the Blessed One said to Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:5.3.2: “Ānanda, the twin Sal trees are in full bloom, even though it’s not the flowering season.
dn16:5.3.3: They shower, strew, & sprinkle on the Tathāgata’s body in homage to him.
dn16:5.3.4: Heavenly coral-tree blossoms are falling from the sky.…
dn16:5.3.5: Heavenly sandalwood powder is falling from the sky.…
dn16:5.3.6: Heavenly music is playing in the sky.…
dn16:5.3.7: Heavenly songs are sung in the sky, in homage to the Tathāgata.
dn16:5.3.8: But it is not to this extent that a Tathāgata is worshipped, honored, respected, venerated, or paid homage to.
dn16:5.3.9: Rather, the monk, nun, male lay follower, or female lay follower who keeps practicing the Dhamma in accordance with the Dhamma, who keeps practicing masterfully, who lives in accordance with the Dhamma:
dn16:5.3.10: That is the person who worships, honors, respects, venerates, & pays homage to the Tathāgata with the highest homage.
dn16:5.3.11: So you should train yourselves: ‘We will keep practicing the Dhamma in accordance with the Dhamma, we will keep practicing masterfully, we will live in accordance with the Dhamma.’ That’s how you should train yourselves.”
dn16:5.4.1: Now on that occasion Ven. Upavāṇa was standing in front of the Blessed One, fanning him.
dn16:5.4.2: Then the Blessed One dismissed him, saying,
dn16:5.4.3: “Go away, monk. Don’t stand in front of me.”
dn16:5.4.4: Then the thought occurred to Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:5.4.5: “For a long time, now, this Ven. Upavāṇa has been an attendant to the Blessed One, staying near him and traveling with him.
dn16:5.4.6: But now, in his final hour, he dismisses him, saying,
dn16:5.4.7: ‘Go away, monk. Don’t stand in front of me.’
dn16:5.4.8: Now what is the reason, what is the cause, why the Blessed One dismisses him, saying,
dn16:5.4.9: ‘Go away, monk. Don’t stand in front of me’?”
dn16:5.5.1: Then Ven. Ānanda said to the Blessed One,
dn16:5.5.2: “For a long time, now, this Ven. Upavāṇa has been an attendant to the Blessed One, staying near him and traveling with him.
dn16:5.5.3: But now, in his final hour, he dismisses him, saying,
dn16:5.5.4: ‘Go away, monk. Don’t stand in front of me.’
dn16:5.5.5: Now what is the reason, what is the cause, why the Blessed One dismisses him, saying,
dn16:5.5.6: ‘Go away, monk. Don’t stand in front of me’?”
dn16:5.5.7: “Ānanda, most of the devatās from ten world-systems have gathered in order to see the Tathāgata.
dn16:5.5.8: For twelve leagues all around Upavattana, the Mallans’ Sal Forest near Kusinārā, there is not the space even of the tip of a horse-tail hair that is not occupied by eminent devatās.
dn16:5.5.10: ‘We have come a long distance to see the Tathāgata.
dn16:5.5.11: Only once in a long, long time does a Tathāgata—worthy & rightly self-awakened—arise in the world.
dn16:5.5.12: Tonight, in the last watch of the night, the total unbinding of the Tathāgata will occur.
dn16:5.5.13: And this eminent monk is standing in front of the Blessed One, blocking the way. We aren’t getting to see the Blessed One in his final hour.’”
dn16:5.6.1: “But, lord, what is the state of the devatās the Blessed One is paying attention to?”
dn16:5.6.2: “Ānanda, there are devatās who perceive space to be earth. Tearing at their hair, they are weeping. Uplifting their arms, they are weeping. As if their feet were cut out from under them, they fall down and roll back & forth, crying,
dn16:5.6.3: ‘All too soon, the Blessed One will totally unbind! All too soon, the One Well-Gone will totally unbind! All too soon, the One with Eyes [alternate reading: the Eye] will disappear from the world!’
dn16:5.6.4: Then there are devatās who perceive earth to be earth. Tearing at their hair, they are weeping. Uplifting their arms, they are weeping. As if their feet were cut out from under them, they fall down and roll back & forth, crying,
dn16:5.6.5: ‘All too soon, the Blessed One will totally unbind! All too soon, the One Well-Gone will totally unbind! All too soon, the One with Eyes will disappear from the world!’
dn16:5.6.6: But those devatās who are free from passion acquiesce, mindful & alert:
dn16:5.6.7: ‘Inconstant are fabrications. What else is there to expect?’”
dn16:5.7.1: “In the past, lord, the monks in all directions, after ending the Rains retreat, came to see the Tathāgata.
dn16:5.7.2: Thus we got to see & attend to the monks who inspire the heart.
dn16:5.7.3: But now, after the Blessed One is gone, we won’t get to see or attend to the monks who inspire the heart.”
dn16:5.8.1: “Ānanda, there are these four places that merit being seen by a clansman with conviction, that merit his feelings of urgency & dismay [saṁvega].
dn16:5.8.3: ‘Here the Tathāgata was born’ is a place that merits being seen by a clansman with conviction, that merits his feelings of urgency & dismay.
dn16:5.8.4: ‘Here the Tathāgata awakened to the unexcelled right self-awakening’.…
dn16:5.8.5: ‘Here the Tathāgata set rolling the unexcelled wheel of Dhamma’.…
dn16:5.8.6: ‘Here the Tathāgata totally unbound in the property of unbinding with no fuel remaining’ is a place that merits being seen by a clansman with conviction, that merits his feelings of urgency & dismay.
dn16:5.8.7: These are the four places that merit being seen by a clansman with conviction, that merit his feelings of urgency & dismay.
dn16:5.8.8: They will come out of conviction, Ānanda—monks, nuns, male lay followers, & female lay followers—to the spots where
dn16:5.8.9: ‘Here the Tathāgata was born,’ ‘Here the Tathāgata awakened to the unexcelled right self-awakening,’ ‘Here the Tathāgata set rolling the unexcelled wheel of Dhamma,’ ‘Here the Tathāgata totally unbound in the property of unbinding with no fuel remaining.’
dn16:5.8.10: And anyone who dies while making a pilgrimage to these shrines with a bright, confident mind will—on the break-up of the body, after death—reappear in a good destination, a heavenly world.”
dn16:5.9.1: “Lord, what course should we follow with regard to womenfolk?”
dn16:5.9.3: “But when there is seeing, lord, what course should be followed?”
dn16:5.9.5: “But when we are addressed, what course should be followed?”
dn16:5.10.1: “And, lord, what course should we follow with regard to the Tathāgata’s body?”
dn16:5.10.2: “You are not to be concerned about the Tathāgata’s funeral.
dn16:5.10.3: Please, Ānanda, strive for the true goal, be committed to the true goal, dwell heedful, ardent, & resolute for the sake of the true goal.
dn16:5.10.4: There are wise nobles, wise brahmans, & wise householders who are highly confident in the Tathāgata. They will conduct the Tathāgata’s funeral.”
dn16:5.11.1: “But, lord, what course should be followed with regard to the Tathāgata’s body?”
dn16:5.11.2: “The course they follow with regard to a wheel-turning monarch’s body, Ānanda, is the course that should be followed with regard to the Tathāgata’s body.”
dn16:5.11.3: “And what, lord, is the course they follow with regard to a wheel-turning monarch’s body?”
dn16:5.11.4: “Ānanda, they wrap the wheel-turning monarch’s body in new linen cloth. Having wrapped it in new linen cloth, they wrap it in teased cotton-wool. Having wrapped it in teased cotton-wool, they wrap it in new linen cloth.
dn16:5.11.5: Having done this five hundred times, they place the body in an iron oil-vat, cover it with an iron lid, make a pyre composed totally of perfumed substances, and cremate the body.
dn16:5.11.6: Then they build a burial mound for the wheel-turning monarch at a great four-way intersection.
dn16:5.11.7: That is the course that they follow with regard to a wheel-turning monarch’s body.
dn16:5.11.8: The course they follow with regard to a wheel-turning monarch’s body, Ānanda, is the course that should be followed with regard to the Tathāgata’s body.
dn16:5.11.9: A burial mound for the Tathāgata is to be built at a great four-way intersection.
dn16:5.11.10: And those who offer a garland, a scent, or a perfume powder there, or bow down there, or brighten their minds there: That will be for their long-term welfare & happiness.
dn16:5.12.1: “There are these four who are worthy of a burial mound.
dn16:5.12.3: A Tathāgata, worthy & rightly self-awakened, is worthy of a burial mound. A Private Buddha… a disciple of a Tathāgata… a wheel-turning monarch is worthy of a burial mound.
dn16:5.12.4: “And for what reason is a Tathāgata, worthy & rightly self-awakened, worthy of a burial mound?
dn16:5.12.5: (At the thought,) ‘This is the burial mound of a Tathāgata, worthy & rightly self-awakened,’ many people will brighten their minds.
dn16:5.12.6: Having brightened their minds there, then—on the break-up of the body, after death—they will reappear in a good destination, a heavenly world.
dn16:5.12.7: It is for this reason that a Tathāgata, worthy & rightly self-awakened, is worthy of a burial mound.
dn16:5.12.8: “And for what reason is a Private Buddha worthy of a burial mound?
dn16:5.12.9: (At the thought,) ‘This is the burial mound of a Private Buddha,’ many people will brighten their minds.
dn16:5.12.10: Having brightened their minds there, then—on the break-up of the body, after death—they will reappear in a good destination, a heavenly world.…
dn16:5.12.12: “And for what reason is a disciple of a Tathāgata worthy of a burial mound?
dn16:5.12.13: (At the thought,) ‘This is the burial mound of a disciple of a Tathāgata,’ many people will brighten their minds.
dn16:5.12.14: Having brightened their minds there, then—on the break-up of the body, after death—they will reappear in a good destination, a heavenly world.…
dn16:5.12.16: “And for what reason is a wheel-turning monarch worthy of a burial mound?
dn16:5.12.17: (At the thought,) ‘This is the burial mound of a wheel-turning monarch,’ many people will brighten their minds.
dn16:5.12.18: Having brightened their minds there, then—on the break-up of the body, after death—they will reappear in a good destination, a heavenly world.
dn16:5.12.19: It is for this reason that a wheel-turning monarch is worthy of a burial mound.
dn16:5.12.20: “These are the four who are worthy of a burial mound.”
dn16:5.13.1: Then Ven. Ānanda, going into a (nearby) building, stood leaning against the door jamb, weeping:
dn16:5.13.2: “Here I am, still in training, with work left to do, and the total unbinding of my teacher is about to occur—the teacher who has had such sympathy for me!”
dn16:5.13.3: Then the Blessed One said to the monks,
dn16:5.13.5: “Lord, Ven. Ānanda, having gone into that building, stands leaning against the door jamb, weeping:
dn16:5.13.6: ‘Here I am, still in training, with work left to do, and the total unbinding of my teacher is about to occur—the teacher who has had such sympathy for me!’”
dn16:5.13.7: Then the Blessed One told a certain monk,
dn16:5.13.8: “Come, monk. In my name, call Ānanda, saying,
dn16:5.13.10: Responding, “As you say, lord,” to the Blessed One, the monk went to Ven. Ānanda and on arrival said,
dn16:5.14.1: Responding, “As you say, my friend,” to the monk, Ven. Ānanda went to the Blessed One and, on arrival, having bowed down to him, sat to one side. As he was sitting there, the Blessed One said to him,
dn16:5.14.2: “Enough, Ānanda. Don’t grieve. Don’t lament. Haven’t I already taught you
dn16:5.14.3: the state of growing different with regard to all things dear & appealing, the state of becoming separate, the state of becoming otherwise?
dn16:5.14.4: What else is there to expect? It’s impossible that one could forbid anything born, existent, fabricated, & subject to disintegration from disintegrating.
dn16:5.14.5: “For a long time, Ānanda, you have waited on the Tathāgata with bodily acts of good will—helpful, happy, whole-hearted, without limit; with verbal acts of good will… with mental acts of good will—helpful, happy, whole-hearted, without limit.
dn16:5.14.6: You are one who has made merit. Commit yourself to exertion, and soon you will be without effluents.”
dn16:5.15.1: Then the Blessed One addressed the monks,
dn16:5.15.2: “Monks, those Blessed Ones who, in the past, were worthy ones, rightly self-awakened, had foremost attendants, just as I have had Ānanda.
dn16:5.15.3: Those Blessed Ones who, in the future, will be worthy ones, rightly self-awakened, will have foremost attendants, just as I have had Ānanda.
dn16:5.15.6: ‘This is the time to approach to see the Tathāgata. This is the time for monks, this the time for nuns, this the time for male lay-followers, this the time for female lay-followers, this the time for kings & their ministers, this the time for sectarians, this the time for the followers of sectarians.
dn16:5.16.1: “There are these four amazing & astounding qualities in Ānanda.
dn16:5.16.3: If a group of monks approaches to see Ānanda, they are gratified at the sight of him.
dn16:5.16.4: If he speaks Dhamma to them, they are gratified with what he says.
dn16:5.16.5: Before they are sated, he falls silent.
dn16:5.16.6: If a group of nuns approaches to see Ānanda…
dn16:5.16.9: If a group of male lay followers approaches to see Ānanda…
dn16:5.16.12: If a group of female lay followers approaches to see Ānanda, they are gratified at the sight of him.
dn16:5.16.13: If he speaks Dhamma to them, they are gratified with what he says.
dn16:5.16.14: Before they are sated, he falls silent.
dn16:5.16.15: These are the four amazing & astounding qualities in Ānanda.
dn16:5.16.16: There are these four amazing & astounding qualities in a wheel-turning monarch.
dn16:5.16.18: If a group of noble warriors approaches to see him…
dn16:5.16.21: If a group of brahmans approaches to see him…
dn16:5.16.22: If a group of householders approaches to see him…
dn16:5.16.23: If a group of contemplatives approaches to see him, they are gratified at the sight of him.
dn16:5.16.24: If he speaks to them, they are gratified with what he says.
dn16:5.16.25: Before they are sated, he falls silent.
dn16:5.16.26: In the same way, monks, there are these four amazing & astounding qualities in Ānanda.
dn16:5.16.32: a group of male lay followers…
dn16:5.16.33: a group of female lay followers approaches to see Ānanda, they are gratified at the sight of him.
dn16:5.16.34: If he speaks Dhamma to them, they are gratified with what he says.
dn16:5.16.37: These are the four amazing & astounding qualities in Ānanda.”
dn16:5.17.1: When this was said, Ven. Ānanda said to the Blessed One,
dn16:5.17.2: “Lord, may the Blessed One not totally unbind in this little town, this dusty town, this branch township.
dn16:5.17.4: Campā, Rājagaha, Sāvatthī, Sāketa, Kosambī, Bārāṇasī.
dn16:5.17.5: May the Blessed One totally unbind there.
dn16:5.17.6: In those cities there are many wealthy noble warriors, brahmans, & householders who have high confidence in the Tathāgata.
dn16:5.17.7: They will conduct the Tathāgata’s funeral.”
dn16:5.17.8: “Don’t say that, Ānanda. Don’t say that:
dn16:5.17.9: ‘this little town, this dusty town, this branch township.’
dn16:5.18.1: In the past, Ānanda, a king named Mahā Sudassana was a wheel-turning monarch, a righteous king ruling righteously, who was a conqueror of the four directions, a stabilizer of his country, endowed with the seven treasures.
dn16:5.18.2: This Kusinārā was his capital city, named Kusāvatī:
dn16:5.18.3: twelve leagues long from east to west, seven leagues wide from north to south.
dn16:5.18.4: Kusāvatī was powerful, rich, & well-populated, crowded with people & prosperous.
dn16:5.18.5: Just as the capital city of the devas named Ālakamandā is powerful, rich, & well-populated, crowded with yakkhas & prosperous;
dn16:5.18.6: in the same way, Kusāvatī was powerful, rich, & well-populated, crowded with people & prosperous.
dn16:5.18.7: By day or by night, it was never lacking in ten sounds:
dn16:5.18.8: the sound of elephants, the sound of horses, the sound of carts, the sound of drums, the sound of tabors, the sound of lutes, the sound of songs, the sound of cymbals, the sound of gongs, with cries of ‘Eat! Drink! Snack!’ as the tenth.
dn16:5.19.1: “Now, Ānanda, go into Kusinārā and announce to the Kusinārā Mallans,
dn16:5.19.2: ‘Tonight, Vāsiṭṭhas, in the last watch of the night, the total unbinding of the Tathāgata will occur.
dn16:5.19.3: Come out, Vāsiṭṭhas! Come out, Vāsiṭṭhas!
dn16:5.19.5: “The Tathāgata’s total unbinding occurred within the borders of our very own town, but we didn’t get to see him in his final hour!”’”
dn16:5.19.6: Responding, “As you say, lord,” to the Blessed One, Ven. Ānanda adjusted his lower robe and—taking his bowl & outer robe—went unaccompanied into Kusinārā.
dn16:5.20.1: Now at that time the Kusinārā Mallans had met for some business in their reception hall.
dn16:5.20.2: Ven. Ānanda went to the reception hall and on arrival announced to them,
dn16:5.20.3: “Tonight, Vāsiṭṭhas, in the last watch of the night, the total unbinding of the Tathāgata will occur.
dn16:5.20.4: Come out, Vāsiṭṭhas! Come out, Vāsiṭṭhas!
dn16:5.20.6: ‘The Tathāgata’s total unbinding occurred within the borders of our very own town, but we didn’t get to see him in his final hour!’”
dn16:5.21.1: When they heard Ven. Ānanda, the Mallans together with their sons, daughters, & wives were shocked, saddened, their minds overflowing with sorrow. Some of them wept, tearing at their hair; they wept, uplifting their arms. As if their feet were cut out from under them, they fell down and rolled back & forth, crying,
dn16:5.21.2: “All too soon, the Blessed One will totally unbind! All too soon, the One Well-Gone will totally unbind! All too soon, the One with Eyes will disappear from the world!”
dn16:5.21.3: Then the Mallans together with their sons, daughters, & wives—shocked, saddened, their minds overflowing with sorrow—went to Ven. Ānanda at Upavattana, the Mallans’ Sal Forest near Kusinārā.
dn16:5.22.1: The thought occurred to Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:5.22.2: “If I let the Mallans pay reverence to the Blessed One one by one, the night will be over before they have finished paying reverence.
dn16:5.22.3: What if I were to have them pay reverence to the Blessed One arranging them family by family, announcing,
dn16:5.22.4: ‘Lord, the Mallan named so-&-so, together with his children & wives, servants & retainers, bows down with his head at the Blessed One’s feet.’”
dn16:5.22.5: So Ven. Ānanda, arranging the Mallans family by family, had them pay reverence to the Blessed One, (saying,)
dn16:5.22.6: “Lord, the Mallan named so-&-so, together with his children & wives, servants & retainers, bows down with his head at the Blessed One’s feet.”
dn16:5.22.7: In this way Ven. Ānanda got the Mallans to pay reverence to the Blessed One within the first watch of the night.
dn16:5.23.1: Now on that occasion a wanderer named Subhadda was staying near Kusinārā.
dn16:5.23.3: “Tonight, in the last watch of the night, the total unbinding of Gotama the contemplative will take place.”
dn16:5.23.4: Then the thought occurred to him:
dn16:5.23.5: “I have heard the old elder wanderers, teachers of teachers, saying that only once in a long, long time do Tathāgatas—worthy ones, rightly self-awakened—appear in the world.
dn16:5.23.6: Tonight, in the last watch of the night, the total unbinding of Gotama the contemplative will occur.
dn16:5.23.7: Now there is a doubt that has arisen in me, but I have confidence in Gotama the contemplative that he can teach me the Dhamma
dn16:5.23.8: in such a way that I might abandon that doubt.”
dn16:5.24.1: So he went to Upavattana, the Mallans’ Sal Forest and, on arrival, said to Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:5.24.2: “I have heard the old elder wanderers, teachers of teachers,
dn16:5.24.3: saying that only once in a long, long time do Tathāgatas—worthy ones, rightly self-awakened—appear in the world.
dn16:5.24.4: Tonight, in the last watch of the night, the total unbinding of Gotama the contemplative will occur.
dn16:5.24.5: Now there is a doubt that has arisen in me,
dn16:5.24.6: but I have confidence in Gotama the contemplative that he can teach me the Dhamma in such a way that I might abandon that doubt.
dn16:5.24.7: It would be good, Ven. Ānanda, if you would let me see him.”
dn16:5.24.8: When this was said, Ven. Ānanda said to him,
dn16:5.24.9: “Enough, friend Subhadda. Don’t bother the Blessed One. The Blessed One is tired.”
dn16:5.24.11: For a third time, Subhadda the wanderer said to Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:5.24.17: “…It would be good, Ven. Ānanda, if you would let me see him.”
dn16:5.24.18: For a third time, Ven. Ānanda said to him,
dn16:5.24.19: “Enough, friend Subhadda. Don’t bother the Blessed One. The Blessed One is tired.”
dn16:5.25.1: Now, the Blessed One heard the exchange between Ven. Ānanda & Subhadda the wanderer,
dn16:5.25.2: and so he said to Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:5.25.3: “Enough, Ānanda. Don’t stand in his way. Let him see the Tathāgata.
dn16:5.25.4: Whatever he asks me will all be for the sake of knowledge, and not to be bothersome.
dn16:5.25.5: And whatever I answer when asked, he will quickly understand.”
dn16:5.25.6: So Ven. Ānanda said to Subhadda the wanderer,
dn16:5.25.7: “Go ahead, friend Subhadda. The Blessed One gives you his leave.”
dn16:5.26.1: Then Subhadda went to the Blessed One and exchanged courteous greetings with him. After an exchange of friendly greetings & courtesies, he sat to one side. As he was sitting there, he said to the Blessed One,
dn16:5.26.2: “Master Gotama, these contemplatives & brahmans, each with his group, each with his community, each the teacher of his group, an honored leader, well-regarded by people at large—
dn16:5.26.3: i.e., Pūraṇa Kassapa, Makkhali Gosāla, Ajita Kesakambalin, Pakudha Kaccāyana, Sañjaya Velaṭṭhaputta, & the Nigaṇṭha Nāṭaputta:
dn16:5.26.4: Do they all have direct knowledge as they themselves claim, or do they all not have direct knowledge, or do some of them have direct knowledge and some of them not?”
dn16:5.26.6: ‘Do they all have direct knowledge as they themselves claim, or do they all not have direct knowledge, or do some of them have direct knowledge and some of them not?’
dn16:5.26.7: I will teach you the Dhamma, Subhadda.
dn16:5.26.8: Listen, and pay close attention. I will speak.”
dn16:5.26.9: “As you say, lord,” Subhadda responded to the Blessed One.
dn16:5.27.1: “In any doctrine & discipline where the noble eightfold path is not ascertained, no contemplative of the first… second… third… fourth order [stream-winner, once-returner, non-returner, or arahant] is ascertained.
dn16:5.27.2: But in any doctrine & discipline where the noble eightfold path is ascertained, contemplatives of the first… second… third… fourth order are ascertained.
dn16:5.27.3: The noble eightfold path is ascertained in this doctrine & discipline, and right here there are contemplatives of the first… second… third… fourth order. Other teachings are empty of knowledgeable contemplatives.
dn16:5.27.4: And if the monks dwell rightly, this world will not be empty of arahants.
dn16:5.27.5: At age twenty-nine I went forth, Subhadda,
dn16:5.27.7: and since my going forth, Subhadda,
dn16:5.27.8: more than fifty years have passed.
dn16:5.27.9: Outside of the realm of methodical Dhamma,
dn16:5.27.11: There is no contemplative of the second order;
dn16:5.27.12: there is no contemplative of the third order;
dn16:5.27.13: there is no contemplative of the fourth order.
dn16:5.27.14: Other teachings are empty of knowledgeable contemplatives.
dn16:5.27.15: And if the monks dwell rightly, this world will not be empty of arahants.”
dn16:5.28.3: Just as if he were to place upright what was overturned, to reveal what was hidden, to show the way to one who was lost, or to set out a lamp in the darkness so that those with eyes could see forms, in the same way has the Blessed One—through many lines of reasoning—made the Dhamma clear.
dn16:5.28.4: I go to the Blessed One for refuge, to the Dhamma, and to the Saṅgha of monks.
dn16:5.28.5: Let me obtain the going forth in the Blessed One’s presence, let me obtain the acceptance.”
dn16:5.29.1: “Anyone, Subhadda, who has previously belonged to another sect and who desires the going forth & acceptance into this Dhamma & Vinaya, must first undergo probation for four months. If, at the end of four months, the monks feel so moved, they give him the going forth & accept him into the monk’s state.
dn16:5.29.2: But I know distinctions among individuals in this matter.”
dn16:5.29.3: “Lord, if that is so, I am willing to undergo probation for four years. If, at the end of four years, the monks feel so moved, let them give me the going forth & accept me into the monk’s state.”
dn16:5.29.4: Then the Blessed One said to Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:5.29.5: “Very well then, Ānanda, give Subhadda the going forth.”
dn16:5.29.6: “As you say, lord,” Ven. Ānanda responded to the Blessed One.
dn16:5.30.1: Then Subhadda said to Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:5.30.2: “It’s a gain for you, Ānanda, a great gain,
dn16:5.30.3: that you have been anointed here, face-to-face with the Teacher, with the pupil’s anointing.”
dn16:5.30.4: Then Subhadda the wanderer obtained the going forth in the Blessed One’s presence, he obtained acceptance.
dn16:5.30.5: And not long after his acceptance—dwelling alone, secluded, heedful, ardent, & resolute—he in no long time entered & remained in the supreme goal of the holy life, for which clansmen rightly go forth from home into homelessness, directly knowing & realizing it for himself in the here & now.
dn16:5.30.6: He knew: “Birth is ended, the holy life fulfilled, the task done. There is nothing further for the sake of this world.”
dn16:5.30.7: And thus Ven. Subhadda became another one of the arahants,
dn16:5.30.8: the last of the Blessed One’s direct-witness disciples.
dn16:6.1.1: Then the Blessed One said to Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:6.1.2: “Now, if the thought occurs to any of you—
dn16:6.1.3: ‘The teaching has lost its arbitrator; we are without a Teacher’—
dn16:6.1.4: do not view it in that way.
dn16:6.1.5: Whatever Dhamma & Vinaya I have pointed out & formulated for you, that will be your Teacher after my passing.
dn16:6.2.1: “At present, the monks address one another as ‘friend,’ but after my passing they are not to address one another that way.
dn16:6.2.2: The more senior monks are to address the newer monks by their name or clan or as ‘friend.’
dn16:6.2.3: The newer monks are to address the more senior monks as ‘venerable’ or ‘sir.’
dn16:6.3.1: “After my passing, the Saṅgha—if it wants—may rescind the lesser & minor training rules.
dn16:6.4.1: “After my passing, the monk Channa should be given the brahma-penalty.”
dn16:6.4.3: “Channa may say what he wants, Ānanda,
dn16:6.4.4: but he is not to be spoken to, instructed, or admonished by the monks.”
dn16:6.5.1: Then the Blessed One addressed the monks,
dn16:6.5.2: “If even a single monk has any doubt or perplexity concerning the Buddha, Dhamma, or Saṅgha, the path or the practice, ask. Don’t later regret that
dn16:6.5.3: ‘The Teacher was face-to-face with us, but we didn’t bring ourselves to ask a counter-question in his presence.’”
dn16:6.5.4: When this was said, the monks were silent.
dn16:6.6.1: A second time, the Blessed One said, “If even one of the monks has any doubt or perplexity concerning the Buddha, Dhamma, or Saṅgha, the path or the practice, ask. Don’t later regret that ‘The Teacher was face-to-face with us, but we didn’t bring ourselves to ask a counter-question in his presence.’” A second time, the monks were silent.
dn16:6.6.2: A third time, the Blessed One said,
dn16:6.6.3: “If even one of the monks has any doubt or perplexity concerning the Buddha, Dhamma, or Saṅgha, the path or the practice, ask. Don’t later regret that
dn16:6.6.4: ‘The Teacher was face-to-face with us, but we didn’t bring ourselves to ask a counter-question in his presence.’”
dn16:6.6.5: A third time, the monks were silent.
dn16:6.6.6: Then the Blessed One addressed the monks,
dn16:6.6.7: “Now, if it’s simply out of respect for the Teacher that you don’t ask, let a companion inform a companion.”
dn16:6.6.8: When this was said, the monks were silent.
dn16:6.6.9: Then Ven. Ānanda said to the Blessed One,
dn16:6.6.10: “It’s amazing, lord. It’s astounding. I have confidence in this Saṅgha of monks that there is not even a single monk in this Saṅgha of monks who has any doubt or perplexity concerning the Buddha, Dhamma, or Saṅgha, the path or the practice.”
dn16:6.6.11: “You, Ānanda, speak out of confidence, while there is knowledge in the Tathāgata that there is not even a single monk in this Saṅgha of monks who has any doubt or perplexity concerning the Buddha, Dhamma, or Saṅgha, the path or the practice.
dn16:6.6.12: Of these 500 monks, the most backward is a stream-winner, never again destined for the lower realms, certain, headed for self-awakening.”
dn16:6.7.1: Then the Blessed One addressed the monks,
dn16:6.7.3: All fabrications are subject to ending & decay. Reach consummation through heedfulness.”
dn16:6.7.4: That was the Tathāgata’s last statement.
dn16:6.8.1: Then the Blessed One entered the first jhāna. Emerging from that he entered the second jhāna. Emerging from that, he entered the third… the fourth jhāna… the dimension of the infinitude of space… the dimension of the infinitude of consciousness… the dimension of nothingness… the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. Emerging from that, he entered the cessation of perception & feeling.
dn16:6.8.2: Then Ven. Ānanda said to Ven. Anuruddha,
dn16:6.8.3: “Ven. Anuruddha,50 the Blessed One is totally unbound.”
dn16:6.8.4: “No, friend Ānanda. The Blessed One isn’t totally unbound. He has entered the cessation of perception & feeling.”
dn16:6.9.1: Then the Blessed One, emerging from the cessation of perception & feeling, entered the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. Emerging from that, he entered the dimension of nothingness… the dimension of the infinitude of consciousness… the dimension of the infinitude of space… the fourth jhāna… the third… the second… the first jhāna. Emerging from the first jhāna he entered the second… the third… the fourth jhāna. Emerging from the fourth jhāna, he immediately totally unbound.
dn16:6.10.1: When the Blessed One totally unbound, simultaneously with the total unbinding, there was a great earthquake, awesome & hair-raising, and rolls of the deva-drums split (the air).
dn16:6.10.2: When the Blessed One totally unbound, simultaneously with the total unbinding, Sahampati Brahmā uttered this verse:
dn16:6.10.4: will cast off the bodily heap
dn16:6.10.5: in the world where a Teacher like this
dn16:6.10.8: the Rightly Self-Awakened One, has totally unbound.
dn16:6.10.9: When the Blessed One totally unbound, simultaneously with the total unbinding, Sakka, the deva-king, uttered this verse:
dn16:6.10.11: Their nature: to arise & pass away.
dn16:6.10.12: They disband as they are arising.
dn16:6.10.14: When the Blessed One totally unbound, simultaneously with the total unbinding, Ven. Anuruddha uttered this verse:
dn16:6.10.16: the firm-minded one, the one who was Such,
dn16:6.10.23: When the Blessed One totally unbound, simultaneously with the total unbinding, Ven. Ānanda uttered this verse:
dn16:6.10.26: when, displaying the foremost accomplishment in all things,
dn16:6.10.27: the Rightly Self-Awakened One totally unbound.
dn16:6.10.28: When the Blessed One totally unbound, simultaneously with the total unbinding, some of the monks present who were not without passion wept, uplifting their arms. As if their feet were cut out from under them, they fell down and rolled back & forth, crying, “All too soon has the Blessed One totally unbound! All too soon has the One Well-Gone totally unbound! All too soon has the One with Eyes disappeared from the world!”
dn16:6.10.29: But those monks who were free from passion acquiesced, mindful & alert:
dn16:6.10.30: “Inconstant are fabrications. What else is there to expect?”
dn16:6.11.1: Then Ven. Anuruddha addressed the monks,
dn16:6.11.3: Hasn’t the Blessed One already taught
dn16:6.11.4: the state of growing different with regard to all things dear & appealing, the state of becoming separate, the state of becoming otherwise?
dn16:6.11.5: What else is there to expect?52 It’s impossible that one could forbid anything born, existent, fabricated, & subject to disintegration from disintegrating.
dn16:6.11.7: [Ven. Ānanda:] “But, Ven. Anuruddha, what is the state of the devatās you are paying attention to?”
dn16:6.11.8: “Friend Ānanda, there are devatās who perceive space to be earth. Tearing at their hair, they are weeping. Uplifting their arms, they are weeping. As if their feet were cut out from under them, they fall down and roll back & forth, crying,
dn16:6.11.9: ‘All too soon has the Blessed One totally unbound! All too soon has the One Well-Gone totally unbound! All too soon, has the One with Eyes disappeared from the world!’
dn16:6.11.10: Then there are devatās who perceive earth to be earth. Tearing at their hair, they are weeping. Uplifting their arms, they are weeping. As if their feet were cut out from under them, they fall down and roll back & forth, crying,
dn16:6.11.11: ‘All too soon has the Blessed One totally unbound! All too soon has the One Well-Gone totally unbound! All too soon has the One with Eyes disappeared from the world!’
dn16:6.11.12: But those devatās who are free from passion acquiesce, mindful & alert:
dn16:6.11.13: ‘Inconstant are fabrications. What else is there to expect?’”
dn16:6.11.14: Then Ven. Anuruddha & Ven. Ānanda spent the remainder of the night in Dhamma talk.
dn16:6.12.1: Then Ven. Anuruddha said to Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:6.12.2: “Go, friend Ānanda. Entering Kusinārā, announce to the Kusinārā Mallans,
dn16:6.12.3: ‘The Blessed One, Vāsiṭṭhas, has totally unbound.
dn16:6.12.4: Now is the time for you to do as you see fit.’”
dn16:6.12.5: Responding, “As you say, sir,” to Ven. Anuruddha, Ven. Ānanda in the early morning adjusted his lower robe and—taking his bowl & outer robe—went unaccompanied into Kusinārā.
dn16:6.12.6: Now at that time the Kusinārā Mallans had met for some business in their reception hall.
dn16:6.12.7: Ven. Ānanda went to the reception hall and on arrival announced to them,
dn16:6.12.8: “The Blessed One, Vāsiṭṭhas, has totally unbound.
dn16:6.12.9: Now is the time for you to do as you see fit.”
dn16:6.12.10: When they heard Ven. Ānanda, the Mallans together with their sons, daughters, & wives were shocked, saddened, their minds overflowing with sorrow. Some of them wept, tearing at their hair; they wept, uplifting their arms. As if their feet were cut out from under them, they fell down and rolled back & forth, crying,
dn16:6.12.11: ”All too soon has the Blessed One totally unbound! All too soon has the One Well-Gone totally unbound! All too soon has the One with Eyes disappeared from the world!”
dn16:6.13.1: Then the Kusinārā Mallans ordered their men,
dn16:6.13.2: “In that case, I say, gather scents, garlands, & all the musical instruments in Kusinārā!”
dn16:6.13.3: Then, taking scents, garlands, & all the musical instruments in Kusinārā, along with 500 pairs of cloth, the Kusinārā Mallans went to the Blessed One’s body in Upavattana, the Mallans’ Sal Forest near Kusinārā. On arrival, they spent the entire day in worshipping, honoring, respecting, & venerating the Blessed One’s body with dances, songs, music, garlands, & scents, in making cloth canopies and arranging floral wreaths.
dn16:6.13.4: Then the thought occurred to them,
dn16:6.13.5: “It’s too late today to cremate the Blessed One’s body. We’ll cremate the Blessed One’s body tomorrow.”
dn16:6.13.6: And so they spent the second day, the third day, the fourth day, the fifth day, the sixth day in worshipping, honoring, respecting, & venerating the Blessed One’s body with dances, songs, music, garlands, & scents, in making cloth canopies and arranging floral wreaths.
dn16:6.14.1: Then on the seventh day the thought occurred to them,
dn16:6.14.2: “Worshipping, honoring, respecting, & venerating the Blessed One’s body with dances, songs, music, garlands, & scents, let’s carry it to the south, around the outside of the town, and cremate it to the south of the town.”
dn16:6.14.3: Then eight leading Mallans, having bathed their heads and wearing new linen cloth, thinking,
dn16:6.14.4: “We’ll lift up the Blessed One’s body,” were unable to lift it.
dn16:6.14.5: So the Kusinārā Mallans asked Ven. Anuruddha,
dn16:6.14.6: “What is the reason, Ven. Anuruddha, what is the cause, why these eight leading Mallans, having bathed their heads and wearing new linen cloth, thinking,
dn16:6.14.7: ‘We’ll lift up the Blessed One’s body,’ are unable to lift it?”
dn16:6.14.8: “Your intention, Vāsiṭṭhas, is one thing. The devas’ intention is another.”
dn16:6.15.1: “But what, Ven. Anuruddha, is the devas’ intention?”
dn16:6.15.3: ‘Worshipping, honoring, respecting, & venerating the Blessed One’s body with dances, songs, music, garlands, & scents, let’s carry it to the south, around the outside of the town, and cremate it to the south of the town.’
dn16:6.15.5: ‘Worshipping, honoring, respecting, & venerating the Blessed One’s body with heavenly dances, songs, music, garlands, & scents, lets carry it to the north of the town, enter the town through the north gate, carry it through the middle of the town and out the east gate to the Mallans’ shrine called Makuṭa-bandhana, to cremate it there.’”
dn16:6.15.6: “Then let it be, venerable sir, in line with the devas’ intention.”
dn16:6.16.1: Now on that occasion Kusinārā—even to its rubbish heaps & cesspools—was strewn knee-deep in coral-tree flowers.
dn16:6.16.2: So the devas & the Kusinārā Mallans, worshipping, honoring, respecting, & venerating the Blessed One’s body with heavenly & human dances, songs, music, garlands, & scents, carried it to the north of the town, entered the town through the north gate, carried it through the middle of the town and out the east gate to the Mallans’ shrine called Makuṭa-bandhana. There they set it down.
dn16:6.17.1: Then the Kusinārā Mallans said to Ven. Ānanda,
dn16:6.17.2: “Venerable sir, what course should we follow with regard to the Tathāgata’s body?”
dn16:6.17.3: “The course they follow with regard to a wheel-turning monarch’s body, Vāsiṭṭhas, is the course that should be followed with regard to the Tathāgata’s body.”
dn16:6.17.4: “And what, venerable sir, is the course they follow with regard to a wheel-turning monarch’s body?”
dn16:6.17.5: “Vāsiṭṭhas, they wrap the wheel-turning monarch’s body in new linen cloth. Having wrapped it in new linen cloth, they wrap it in teased cotton-wool. Having wrapped it in teased cotton-wool, they wrap it in new linen cloth.
dn16:6.17.6: Having done this five hundred times, they place the body in an iron oil-vat, cover it with an iron lid, make a pyre composed totally of perfumed substances, and cremate the body.
dn16:6.17.7: Then they build a burial mound for the wheel-turning monarch at a great four-way intersection.
dn16:6.17.8: That is the course that they follow with regard to the wheel-turning monarch’s body.
dn16:6.17.9: The course they follow with regard to a wheel-turning monarch’s body, Vāsiṭṭhas, is the course that should be followed with regard to the Tathāgata’s body.
dn16:6.17.10: A burial mound for the Tathāgata is to be built at a great, four-way intersection.
dn16:6.17.11: And those who offer a garland, a scent, or a perfume powder there, or bow down there, or brighten their minds there:
dn16:6.18.1: that will be for their long-term welfare & happiness.”
dn16:6.18.2: So the Kusinārā Mallans ordered their men, “In that case, I say, gather the Mallans’ teased cotton-wool.”
dn16:6.18.3: Then they wrapped the Blessed One’s body in new linen cloth. Having wrapped it in new linen cloth, they wrapped it in teased cotton-wool. Having wrapped it in teased cotton-wool, they wrapped it in new linen cloth.
dn16:6.18.4: Having done this five hundred times, they placed the body in an iron oil-vat, covered it with an iron lid, made a pyre composed totally of perfumed substances, and set the body on the pyre.
dn16:6.19.1: Now on that occasion Ven. Mahā Kassapa was traveling on the highway from Pāvā to Kusinārā with a large Saṅgha of monks, approximately 500 monks in all.
dn16:6.19.2: Leaving the road, he sat down at the root of a tree.
dn16:6.19.3: Meanwhile, a certain Ājīvakan ascetic, carrying a coral-tree flower from Kusinārā, was traveling on the highway to Pāvā.
dn16:6.19.4: Ven. Mahā Kassapa saw the Ājīvakan ascetic coming from afar, and on seeing him said to him,
dn16:6.19.6: “Yes, friend, I know him. Seven days ago Gotama the contemplative totally unbound.
dn16:6.19.7: That’s how I got this coral-tree flower.”
dn16:6.19.8: With that, some of the monks present who were not without passion wept, uplifting their arms. As if their feet were cut out from under them, they fell down and rolled back & forth, crying,
dn16:6.19.9: “All too soon has the Blessed One totally unbound! All too soon has the One Well-Gone totally unbound! All too soon has the One with Eyes disappeared from the world!”
dn16:6.19.10: But those monks who were free from passion acquiesced, mindful & alert:
dn16:6.19.11: “Inconstant are fabrications. What else is there to expect?”
dn16:6.20.1: Now at that time a monk named Subhadda,54 who had gone forth when old, was sitting among the group.
dn16:6.20.3: “Enough, friends. Don’t grieve. Don’t lament. We’re well rid of the Great Contemplative. We’ve been harassed by (his saying,)
dn16:6.20.4: ‘This is allowable. This is not allowable.’
dn16:6.20.5: But now we will do what we want to do, and not do what we don’t want to do.”
dn16:6.20.6: Then Ven. Mahā Kassapa addressed the monks,
dn16:6.20.8: Hasn’t the Blessed One already taught the state of growing different with regard to all things dear & appealing,
dn16:6.20.9: the state of becoming separate, the state of becoming otherwise?
dn16:6.20.10: What else is there to expect? It’s impossible that one could forbid anything born, existent, fabricated, & subject to disintegration from disintegrating.”
dn16:6.21.1: Then four leading Mallans, having bathed their heads and wearing new linen cloth, thinking,
dn16:6.21.2: “We’ll ignite the Blessed One’s pyre,” were unable to ignite it.
dn16:6.21.3: So the Kusinārā Mallans asked Ven. Anuruddha,
dn16:6.21.4: “What is the reason, Ven. Anuruddha, what is the cause, why these four leading Mallans, having bathed their heads and wearing new linen cloth, thinking,
dn16:6.21.5: ‘We’ll ignite the Blessed One’s pyre,’ are unable to ignite it?”
dn16:6.21.6: “Your intention, Vāsiṭṭhas, is one thing. The devas’ intention is another.”
dn16:6.21.7: “But what, Ven. Anuruddha, is the devas’ intention?”
dn16:6.21.9: ‘This Ven. Mahā Kassapa is traveling on the highway from Pāvā to Kusinārā with a large Saṅgha of monks, approximately 500 monks in all.
dn16:6.21.10: The Blessed One’s pyre will not catch fire until Ven. Mahā Kassapa has worshipped the Blessed One’s feet with his bathed head.’”
dn16:6.21.11: “Then let it be, venerable sir, in line with the devas’ intention.”
dn16:6.22.1: So Ven. Mahā Kassapa went to the Blessed One’s pyre at Makuṭa-bandhana, the Mallans’ memorial near Kusinārā. On arrival, arranging his robe over one shoulder, he placed his hands palm-to-palm over his heart, circumambulated the pyre three times, uncovered the Blessed One’s feet, and worshipped them with his head.
dn16:6.22.2: And the 500 monks, arranging their robes over one shoulder, placed their hands palm-to-palm over their hearts, circumambulated the pyre three times, and worshipped the Blessed One’s feet with their heads.
dn16:6.22.3: As soon as it had been worshipped by Ven. Mahā Kassapa and the 500 monks, the Blessed One’s pyre caught fire of its own accord.
dn16:6.23.1: In the burning of the Blessed One’s body, no cinder or ash of the outer skin, inner skin, flesh, tendons, or oil of the joints could be discerned.
dn16:6.23.3: Just as in the burning of ghee or oil, no cinder or ash can be discerned;
dn16:6.23.4: in the same way, in the burning of the Blessed One’s body, no cinder or ash of the outer skin, inner skin, flesh, tendons, or oil of the joints could be discerned.
dn16:6.23.6: And of the five hundred twin-wrappings, only two were burnt: the innermost & the outermost.
dn16:6.23.7: When the Blessed One’s body was consumed, a cascade of water falling from the sky extinguished [nibbāpesi] the Blessed One’s pyre.
dn16:6.23.8: Water shooting up from a Sal tree as well extinguished the Blessed One’s pyre.
dn16:6.23.9: The Kusinārā Mallans, with all kinds of scented water, extinguished the Blessed One’s pyre.
dn16:6.23.10: Then for seven days the Kusinārā Mallans kept the bone-relics in their reception hall—setting them round with a lattice of spears surrounded by ramparts of bows—worshipping, honoring, respecting, & venerating them with dances, songs, music, garlands, & scents.
dn16:6.24.1: Then King Ajātasattu Vedehiputta of Magadha heard,
dn16:6.24.2: “The Blessed One, they say, has totally unbound in Kusinārā.”
dn16:6.24.3: So he sent an envoy to the Kusinārā Mallans:
dn16:6.24.4: “The Blessed One was a noble warrior. I, too, am a noble warrior. I deserve a share of the Blessed One’s bone-relics. I, too, will build a burial mound and hold a ceremony for them.”
dn16:6.24.6: “The Blessed One, they say, has totally unbound in Kusinārā.”
dn16:6.24.7: So they sent an envoy to the Kusinārā Mallans:
dn16:6.24.8: “The Blessed One was a noble warrior. We, too, are noble warriors. We deserve a share of the Blessed One’s bone-relics. We, too, will build a burial mound and hold a ceremony for them.”
dn16:6.24.10: “The Blessed One, they say, has totally unbound in Kusinārā.”
dn16:6.24.11: So they sent an envoy to the Kusinārā Mallans:
dn16:6.24.12: “The Blessed One was the greatest of our relatives. We deserve a share of the Blessed One’s bone-relics. We, too, will build a burial mound and hold a ceremony for them.”
dn16:6.24.18: “The Blessed One, they say, has totally unbound in Kusinārā.”
dn16:6.24.19: So they sent an envoy to the Kusinārā Mallans:
dn16:6.24.20: “The Blessed One was a noble warrior. We, too, are noble warriors. We deserve a share of the Blessed One’s bone-relics. We, too, will build a burial mound and hold a ceremony for them.”
dn16:6.24.21: The brahman of Veṭṭha Island heard,
dn16:6.24.22: “The Blessed One, they say, has totally unbound in Kusinārā.”
dn16:6.24.23: So he sent an envoy to the Kusinārā Mallans:
dn16:6.24.24: “The Blessed One was a noble warrior. I am a brahman. I deserve a share of the Blessed One’s bone-relics. I, too, will build a burial mound and hold a ceremony for them.”
dn16:6.24.26: “The Blessed One, they say, has totally unbound in Kusinārā.”
dn16:6.24.27: So they sent an envoy to the Kusinārā Mallans:
dn16:6.24.28: “The Blessed One was a noble warrior. We, too, are noble warriors. We deserve a share of the Blessed One’s bone-relics. We, too, will build a burial mound and hold a ceremony for them.”
dn16:6.25.1: When this was said, the Kusinārā Mallans said to the groups & factions,
dn16:6.25.2: “The Blessed One totally unbound within the borders of our own town. We will not give up a share of the Blessed One’s bone-relics.”
dn16:6.25.3: When this was said, Doṇa the brahman addressed the groups & factions,
dn16:6.25.4: “Listen, good sirs, to a word from me.
dn16:6.25.5: Our Awakened One was a teacher of forbearance.
dn16:6.25.6: It’s not good that there should be combat
dn16:6.25.7: over the sharing of the relics of the highest person.
dn16:6.25.8: Let us, masters, unite in concord,
dn16:6.25.9: on friendly terms, and make eight shares.
dn16:6.25.10: Let there be burial mounds in the various directions,
dn16:6.25.11: many people made confident in the One with Eyes.”
dn16:6.25.12: “In that case, brahman, you yourself divide the Blessed One’s bone-relics into eight equal shares.”
dn16:6.25.13: Responding, “As you say, good sirs,” to the groups & factions, Doṇa the brahman divided the Blessed One’s bone-relics into eight equal shares and then said to the groups & factions,
dn16:6.25.14: “Good sirs, give me this urn. I will build a burial mound and hold a ceremony for the urn.”
dn16:6.26.1: Then the Moriyans of Pipphalivana heard,
dn16:6.26.2: “The Blessed One, they say, has totally unbound in Kusinārā.”
dn16:6.26.3: So they sent an envoy to the Kusinārā Mallans:
dn16:6.26.4: “The Blessed One was a noble warrior. We, too, are noble warriors. We deserve a share of the Blessed One’s bone-relics. We, too, will build a burial mound and hold a ceremony for them.”
dn16:6.26.5: “There is no (remaining) share of the Blessed One’s bone-relics. They have been divided.
dn16:6.26.7: They took the ashes from there.
dn16:6.27.1: Then King Ajātasattu Vedehiputta of Magadha built a burial mound and held a ceremony for the Blessed One’s relics in Rājagaha.
dn16:6.27.2: The Licchavis of Vesālī built a burial mound and held a ceremony for the Blessed One’s relics in Vesālī.
dn16:6.27.3: The Sakyans of Kapilavatthu built a burial mound and held a ceremony for the Blessed One’s relics in Kapilavatthu.
dn16:6.27.4: The Thulayans of Allakappa built a burial mound and held a ceremony for the Blessed One’s relics in Allakappa.
dn16:6.27.5: The Koliyans of Rāmagāma built a burial mound and held a ceremony for the Blessed One’s relics in Rāmagāma.
dn16:6.27.6: The brahman of Veṭṭha Island built a burial mound and held a ceremony for the Blessed One’s relics on Veṭṭha Island.
dn16:6.27.7: The Pāvā Mallans built a burial mound and held a ceremony for the Blessed One’s relics in Pāvā.
dn16:6.27.8: The Kusinārā Mallans built a burial mound and held a ceremony for the Blessed One’s relics in Kusinārā.
dn16:6.27.9: Doṇa the brahman built a burial mound and held a ceremony for the urn.
dn16:6.27.10: The Moriyans of Pipphalivana built a burial mound and held a ceremony for the embers in Pipphalivana.
dn16:6.27.11: Thus there were eight burial mounds for the bone-relics, a ninth for the urn, and a tenth for the embers.
dn16:6.27.12: That is how it was in the past.
dn16:6.28.1: Eight portions were the relics of the One with Eyes,
dn16:6.28.2: the highest, the foremost of men:
dn16:6.28.4: and one in Rāmagāma honored by kings of the nāgas.
dn16:6.28.5: One tooth the Devas of the Thirty-three worship;
dn16:6.28.7: one in the realm of the king of Kāliṅga;
dn16:6.28.8: another is honored by kings of the nāgas.
dn16:6.28.9: These, with their splendor, their excellent gifts,
dn16:6.28.11: Thus the relics of the One with Eyes
dn16:6.28.12: are honored by those honored by those who are honored.
dn16:6.28.13: He is worshiped by deva kings, nāga kings, human kings,
dn16:6.28.14: and likewise is worshiped by the most excellent people.
dn16:6.28.15: So pay homage to him, with hands palm-to-palm over the heart,
dn16:6.28.16: for the Awakened are rarely encountered in the course of one hundred eons.
